All nouns in French are either masculine or feminine. And grammatical elements such as articles and adjectives are required to go agree with the gender of the noun.


NounArticle + Noun

m.

Français

French person

f.

Some nouns that refer to people or occupations change their form when referring to males or females.

200021.Value:
1. Some nouns share the same form in both genders, such as "professeur (teacher)", so their genders are indicated by the articles "le/la/un/une". 

2. In French, it's grammatically correct to put the profession directly after "... être"; an article is not required here.

For example: Je suis professeur. / I am a teacher. (lit. I am teacher.)

200023.Value:
In French, it's grammatically correct to put the profession directly after "pronoun/name + être"; an article is not required here.

For example: Je suis étudiant. (I am a student.)

le/la → l’:

For example, "l’homme" and "l’enfant"

200537.Value:
How to negate a French verb: "ne + verb + pas"

PositiveNegative

Je suis professeur.

I am a teacher.

Je ne suis pas professeur.

I am not a teacher.

Elle boit du thé.

She drinks tea.

Elle ne boit pas de thé.

She doesn’t drink tea.

Il est avocat.

He is a lawyer.

Il n’est pas avocat.

He is not a lawyer.

Tu aimes la baguette.

You like baguette.

Tu n’aimes pas la baguette.

You don’t like baguette.

However, if the negated verb has a vowel or H muet as the first letter, "ne" needs elision to become "n’".

200547.Value:
Verb Conjugation in the Present: "aimer" (to like/love)

Subject Pronoun
Aimer
Example

je

I

aime

J’aime le café.

I like coffee.

tu

you

aimes

Tu aimes le melon.

You like melons.

il/elle

he/she

aime

Il aime la viande.

He likes meat.

Attention:

(1) Don’t forget that "je" becomes j’ when it precedes a verb starting with a vowel or H muet.

(2) Contrary to English, "le/la + singular noun" in French can also refer to a group of objects in a general sense. "Le café" can refer to coffee as a type of beverage in general. When we talk about what we love/hate, it's usually a general concept, so we use "le/la + singular noun". 

E.g.:

  • J'aime le café.  (I like coffee.)
  • Je n'aime pas le café. (I don't like coffee.)

200614.Value:
French Surnames with "de"

In the old days, a surname with "de" would tell people that you are from a noble family. Nowadays, a surname with "de" still implies the possibility of a noble family root, but it is not a guarantee anymore.

(Closest to the speaker)

(Relatively further away from the speaker, a vague reference; colloquially though, "là" is often used interchangeably with "ici". To avoid causing confusion for beginners, we are not including this usage in this unit.)

(Far from the speaker, an exact location)

Examples :

Location AdverbExample

ici

I am here

là

Tu es là.

You are there.

Ils ne sont pas là.

They (male) are not there.

là-bas

Là-bas c’est un bar.

That is a bar over there.

J’aime ce café-là, je bois du café là-bas.

I like that café over there, I drink coffee there.


Demonstrative Adjectives

Demonstrative adjectives are words like "this", "that", "those". 

They agree with the noun they refer to in both gender and number. Therefore, there is more than one form of demonstrative adjectives in French.

Attention:

1) When the demonstrative adjective for masculine singular nouns "ce" appears before a noun starting with a vowel or H muet, for the convenience of pronunciation, "ce" takes the form "cet":

- cet ordinateur (this computer)

- cet enfant (this child)

2) "Ce", "cette", "cet" and "ces" can mean "this" as well as "that". To distinguish, "-là" is attached to the noun to indicate "that".

200730.Value:
Besides indicating ownership or possession, "de" can indicate the quality/nature of someone or something. The noun denoting the quality does not need an article.

Example

les cours de français

French courses

Vous avez des cours d’anglais.

You have English courses.

J’aime le professeur de français.

I like the French teacher. (the teacher who teaches French)

200735.Value:
The expression "... venir de + place" means "to come from somewhere". 

1) To come from a city, no article:

CityExample

Paris

Je viens de Paris.

I come from Paris.

Toulouse

Adrien vient de Toulouse.

Adrien comes from Toulouse.

2) To come from a country whose proper noun is feminine, no article:

CountryExample

(la) France

Nous venons de France.

We come from France.

(l’) Italie

Les filles viennent d’Italie.

The girls come from Italy.

(l’) Espagne

Ce singe vient d’Espagne.

This monkey comes from Spain.

200746.Value:
When the country name is a feminine noun, we use "en" directly before the country name:

Example

Nous sommes en France.

We are in France.

Es-tu en Allemagne?

Are you in Germany?

200747.Value:
To say "in [country name]", if the country name is a plural noun, we need to use the preposition "aux":

Example

Les musiciens sont aux Etats-Unis.

The musicians are in the US.

Cet homme travaille aux Pays-Bas.

This man works in the Netherlands.

200750.Value:
In French, nationality nouns and adjectives share the same spelling. The only difference between a nationality noun and an adjective in French is that the noun has the first letter capitalized, whereas the adjective doesn't.

For example:

  • Français (Frenchman) - C’est un Français. (This is a Frenchman.)
  • français (French) - Il est français. (He is French.)

Attention: In practice, when the subject is a pronoun, French people use the adjective to talk about nationality, as in "someone is adj.".

Wrong: Elle est une Française. (She is a French woman.)
Correct: Elle est française. (She is French.)

200751.Value:
French direct object pronouns come BEFORE the verb. For example, instead of "I love you", the French word order would be "I you love".

When the verb that follows the direct object pronoun starts with a vowel or H muet, elision is necessary. 

The plural direct object pronouns do not differ in gender.

200776.Value:
Interrogative Adjective "Quel" (which):

"quel" is an interrogative adjective, and it should used with a noun, except in "quel + être" fixed expression.

Structure: Quel + noun + verb + subject ?

Example

Quel jour sommes-nous ?

What day is it today? 

(lit. Which day are we?)

Quelle heure est-il ?

What time is it?

(lit. Which hour is it?)

Quelle fille est ta sœur ?

Which girl is your sister?

Quel homme est votre père ?

Which man is your (formal you) father?

200818.Value:
Subject + verb + à + specific time

(Attention: "Midi" and "minuit" are considered specific times in French.)

Example

Je viens à treize heures.

I come at 13 o’clock (1 PM).

Il ne travaille pas à minuit.

He doesn’t work at midnight.

Nous finissons à sept heures et demie.

We finish at half past seven.

Je vais à la banque à midi.

I go to the bank at noon.

200819.Value:
avant de + inf.

Example

Avant d’aller en France, tu veux apprendre le français ?

Before going to France, do you want to learn French?

Avant de venir ici, tu dois finir ton travail.

Before coming here, you should finish your work.

200868.Value:
When negating a noun that contains a plural concept (e.g. shoes, socks, glasses), we use the plural form of the noun in negation.

Example

Je ne porte pas de chaussettes.

I don’t wear shoes.

Elle ne porte pas de lunettes.

I don’t wear glasses.

Il n’y pas pas de saisons ici.

There are no seasons here.

200884.Value:
The question word (interrogative pronoun) "combien" means "how many".

This is how we put it in practice:

Combien + de + n.(pl.) + v. + - + subject

QuestionAnswer

Combien de jupes as-tu ?

How many skirts do you have?

Je n’ai pas de jupes.

I don’t have skirts (at all).

Combien de frères as-tu ?

How many brothers do you have?

J’ai deux frères.

I have two brothers.

Combien de chiens a-t-elle ?

How many dogs does she have?

Elle a un chien.

She has a dog.

Combien de lunettes ont-ils ?

How many glasses do they (male) have?

Ils n’ont pas de lunettes.

They (male) don’t have glasses (at all).

200901.Value: For exact multiples of the hundred (200, 500, etc.), "cent" needs "-s", as in "deux cents" and "trois cents". When it's not the exact multiples of 100, lose the "-s".

200905.Value:
Impersonal Pronoun "Ça" (it/this)

1) Normally, "ça" is used colloquially to refer to an object. We don’t use it on people.

Example

Ça coûte combien ?

How much does it cost?

J’aime ça !

I like this!

Ça, ça et ça, je les achète !

This, this and this, I (will) take (buy) them all!

2) It is mandatory in some fixed expressions to use "ça":

Example

A: Ça va ?

How are you?

B: Ça va. Et toi, ça va ?

I’m fine. You? How are you?

A: Ça va.

Good.

C’est bien ça !

Good!

Ça y est !

That’s it!

200906.Value: This is a fixed expression. The adjective-noun gender and number agreement rule doesn’t apply to "bon marché".

200917.Value: To say "the second grade" in French, we can only use "seconde", not "deuxième".

200918.Value: An ordinal number is always feminine when it refers to "Xth grade" in French.

200921.Value:
"Lequel" means "which one(s)", and it must agree in both gender and number with the noun it refers to.

Example

Laquelle de ces filles est ta fille ?

Which one of these girls is your daughter?

Attention: "Lequel" is an interrogative pronoun, "quel" is an interrogative adjective. Therefore, in standard usage, "lequel" can be used on its own, while "quel" must be used with a noun.

Lequel
Quel

Laquelle est ma chemise ?

Which one is my shirt?

Quelle chemise est ma chemise ?

Which shirt is my shirt?

200922.Value:
"Lequel" means "which one(s)", and it must agree in both gender and number with the noun it refers to.

Example

Il y a des stylos ici, lequel est mon stylo ?

There are some pens here, which one is my pen?

Attention: "Lequel" is an interrogative pronoun, "quel" is an interrogative adjective. Therefore, in standard usage, "lequel" can be used on its own, while "quel" must be used with a noun.

Lequel
Quel

Lequel est mon professeur ?

Which one is my teacher?

Quel homme est mon professeur ?

Which man is my teacher?

200933.Value:
"1st" is "premier" for masculine nouns and "première" for feminine nouns.

For numbers like "21st, 51st or 71st", the "-1st" is "et unième" instead of "premier".

200934.Value:
"Car" is used to connect the result and the cause. So both the consequence and the reason must be present in the sentence.

Example

Je ne t’aime pas, car je te déteste.

I don’t like you, because I hate you.

Je travaille ici, car j’aime mon travail.

I work here, because I like my job.

200947.Value:
Using Adverbial Pronoun "y"

1) To replace any adverbial phrase with "à/chez/dans/devant/sur + place/location":

ExampleTu es au bar, j’y suis aussi.（y=au bar）

 You're at the bar. I'm in it, too.

J’aime ce roman, j’y ai trouvé des choses intéressantes.（y=dans ce roman）

I like this novel. I found something interesting in it.


2) To replace any indirect object led by "à" except humans:

ExampleCette question est difficile, j’y réfléchis. （y=réfléchis à cette quesiton）

This question is hard. I'm thinking about it.

Cette lettre, il faut y répondre. （y=répondre à cette lettre）

This letter, it should be answered. 

Attention : "Y" can’t be used to refer to humans.

Attention:

The stressed personal pronouns "nous/vous" look identical to the subject pronouns, but their grammatical functions are not even close.

In this unit, we touched on only the first and second person stressed pronouns: 

SPP = stressed personal pronoun

SPPExample

moi

Tu es devant moi.

You are in front of me.

nous

Il est à côté de nous.

He is next to us.

toi

Je suis à gauche de toi.

I am on your left.

vous

Elle est à droite de vous.

She is on your right.

200959.Value:
Ownership/Possession: Être + À + Stressed Personal Pronouns

Besides "de", we can also use this expression formula to indicate ownership/possession. 

Example

Ces chaussures sont à moi.

These shoes are mine.

Cette maison est à toi.

This house is yours.

Mon frère, cette banque est à nous.

My brother, this bank is ours.

Attention:

(1) Don’t forget that "je" becomes j’ when it precedes a verb starting with a vowel or H muet.

(2) Contrary to English, "le/la + singular noun" in French can also refer to a group of objects in a general sense. "Le café" can refer to coffee as a type of beverage in general.

When we talk about what we love/hate, it's usually a general concept, so we use "le/la + singular noun".

200992.Value:
"En"

This preposition is used to talk about means of transport. "En" is paired with vehicles.

Example ("en")

Je vais à l’école en vélo.

I bike to school.

Nous allons à la gare en métro.

We take the subway to the train station.

Ils vont en France en avion et en train.

They (male) go to France by plane and by train.

201004.Value:
Here are two ways to apply "jouer":

UsageExample

jouer + à + sport/game

Jouer au football

to play soccer

Je joue aux cartes.

I play cards.

Nous n’aimons pas jouer aux échecs.

We don’t like playing chess.

jouer + de + musical instrument

Jouer de la guitare

to play the guitar

Elle joue du piano.

She plays the piano.

Adrien aime le jazz, il joue de la guitare.

Adrien likes jazz, he plays the guitar.

201008.Value:
Verb "Jouer" (to play)

Here are two ways to apply "jouer":

UsageExample

jouer + à + sport/game

Jouer au football

to play soccer

Je joue aux cartes.

I play cards.

Nous n’aimons pas jouer aux échecs.

We don’t like playing chess.

jouer + de + musical instrument

Jouer de la guitare

to play the guitar

Elle joue du piano.

She plays the piano.

Adrien aime le jazz, il joue de la guitare.

Adrien likes jazz, he plays the guitar.

201014.Value:
Verb Conjugation in the Present Tense: "Savoir" (to know/can)

Savoir

je sais

I know/can

tu sais

you know/can

il/elle sait

he/she/it knows/can

nous savons

we know/can

vous savez

you know/can

ils/elles savent

they know/can

2. Using "Savoir"

"Savoir" can mean "to know" and "can/to be able to".

The message it conveys is determined by what part of speech it goes with.

UsageExample

savoir + n.

to know

Je sais Paris.

I know Paris.

Mon chat sais le chemin.

My cat knows the way.

savoir + inf.

can/to be able to

Je sais jouer du piano.

I can play the piano.

Cette poisson sait nager, ce coq aussi !

This fish can swim, so can this rooster!

Mon père sait bien skier.

My father can ski well.


Ma mère ne sait pas faire la cuisine.

My mother can’t cook.

used alone: 

savoir

to know

Je sais.

I know.

Qui sait…

Who knows…

201016.Value:
When the indefinite article "des" precedes a plural adjective that comes before a noun, "des" becomes "de". 

Example

de jolies filles

some pretty girls

de jeunes étudiants 

some young students

"des" does not become "de" when the adjective follows the modified noun.

Wrong: Ce sont de musiciens connus.
Correct: Ce sont des musiciens connus.

201031.Value:
Verb "Penser" (to think)

As learned in the lesson, when "penser" goes with "à", the combination means "to miss" or "to think of/about".

Penser + à

Penser à la famille

to miss home

Je pense à toi.

I miss you.

Je pense à envoyer une lettre.

I’m thinking of sending a letter.

201032.Value:
Only transitive-direct verbs (transitive verbs that take direct objects) can be made passive.

A + être + past participle + par/de + B

(A is done by B)

Attention: 

The past participle must agree in both gender and number with the subject "A".

Let’s take a look at a comparison between the active and the passive:

ActivePassive

Julie invite Adrien.

(present)

Julie invites Adrien.

Adrien est invité par Julie.

Adrien is invited by Julie.

Adrien invite Julie.

(present)

Adrien invites Julie.

Julie est invitée par Adrien.

Julie is invited by Adrien.

Adrien a invité Julie.

(passé composé)

Adrien has invited Julie.

Julie a été invitée par Adrien.

Julie has been invited by Adrien.


Je suis aussi contente d’être invitée.

(infinitive)

I'm glad to be invited. (female speaker)

201036.Value:
The basic structures of comparatives are fixed:

A is more...

A + être + plus/moins + adj.

Je suis plus grand.

I’m taller.

A is more…than B

A + être + plus/moins + adj. + que + B

Je suis plus grand que mon frère.

I’m taller than my brother.

201038.Value:
Some adjectives ending in "-s" change the ending to "-sse" when they modify feminine nouns.

m.f.

bas

low

basse

low

gros

big/large/fat

grosse

big/large/fat

201041.Value:
The basic structures of comparatives are fixed:

A is more...

A + être + plus/moins + adj.

Tu es moins grand.

You’re shorter.

(lit. You are less tall.)

A is more…than B

A + être + plus/moins + adj. + que + B

Mon frère est moins grand que moi.

My brother is shorter (less tall) than me.

201049.Value:
The comparatives of "bon" (good) and "mauvais" (bad) are irregular, just like "better" and "worse".

"Better" is "meilleur".

Example

Ce restaurant est meilleur.

This restaurant is better.

201050.Value:
The comparatives of "bon" (good) and "mauvais" (bad) are irregular, just like "better" and "worse".

"Worse" is "pire".

Example

Ce n’est pas une nouvelle mauvaise. Cette nouvelle est pire.

This is not bad news. It’s worse.

201070.Value:
Être + Adj. + De + Inf. ...

This expression formula means "to be...to do/for something".

Let’s see some examples to get a better idea:

Example

Je suis content de te voir.

I am happy to see you.

Il est triste de ne pas passer l’examen.

He is upset about not passing the exam.

Nous ne sommes pas content d’aller à l’école.

We are not happy about going to school.

Attention:

If the infinitive part delivers a negative message, "ne pas" comes BEFORE the infinitive. (see the 2nd example)

However, if the part before "de" has negation, "ne pas" surrounds the verb. (see the 3rd example)

201075.Value:
Using "de" in Negation

Generally, in negative sentences, after the negation part, for example after "ne…pas", if the direct object is preceded by an indefinite article or a partitive article, they will be replaced with "de". 

Positive StatementNegative Statement

Je bois du soda.

I drink soda.

Je ne bois pas de soda.

I don’t drink soda (at all).

Tu manges une baguette.

You eat a baguette.

Tu ne manges pas de baguette.

You don’t eat baguettes (at all).

Elle boit du thé.

She drinks tea.

Elle ne boit pas de thé.

She doesn’t drink soda (at all).

Attention:

1) Using "de" to negate indicates absolute negation. Take "Je ne bois pas de soda" as an example. It means "I don’t drink soda at all". 

2) In negation, "de" can only take the place of an indefinite article or a partitive one, never a definite article:

Je n’aime pas le soda. (✔)

Je n’aime pas de soda. (✖)

Elle n’aime pas la viande. (✔)

Elle n’aime pas de viande. (✖)

201100.Value:
To Need To Do Something

The expression "avoir besoin de faire" means "to need to do".

"Faire" here means infinitives.

Example

J’ai besoin d’apprendre le français.

I need to learn French.

Nous avons besoin d’aller à l’hôpital.

We need to go to the hospital.

Mon grand-père n’a plus besoin de travailler.

My grandfather doesn’t need to work anymore.

201118.Value:
To Desire to Do Something

By using "avoir envie de faire", we express a strong desire to do something.

Example

J’ai envie d’apprendre le français.

I want to learn French. 

Nous avons envie de voyager en Espagne.

We want to travel to Spain.

Mon grand-père n’a plus envie de travailler.

My grandfather doesn't want to work anymore.

201121.Value:
Adjective "Nouveau" (new)

Forms of "Nouveau"Example

nouveau + n.(m. sg.)

When the noun starts with any letter other than a vowel or H muet

Je veux acheter un nouveau stylo.

I want to buy a new pen.

nouvel + n.(m. sg.)

When the noun starts with a vowel or H muet

Mon nouvel étudiant est intelligent.

My new student is smart.

nouvelle + n. (f. sg.)

All feminine singular nouns.

Elle aime bien sa nouvelle robe.

She likes her new dress.

nouveaux + n.(m. pl.)

Ma mère veut acheter toujours de nouveaux sacs.

My mother always wants to buy new bags.

nouvelles + n.(f. pl.)

Ce sont de nouvelles chaussures.

These are some new shoes.

Forms of "Nouveau"Example

nouveau + n.(m. sg.)

When the noun starts with any letter other than a vowel or H muet

Je veux acheter un nouveau stylo.

nouvel + n.(m. sg.)

When the noun starts with a vowel or H muet

Mon nouvel étudiant est intelligent.

nouvelle + n. (f. sg.)

All feminine singular nouns.

These are some new shoes.

201160.Value:
Des + Plural Adjective = De

When the indefinite article "des" comes before an adjective in its plural form, "des" will become "de". 

However, this applies to only one circumstance where the adjective is BEFORE the noun.

Example

de jolies filles

some pretty girls

de jeunes étudiants 

some young students

In the following example, "des" does not become "de" because the adjective succeeds the modified noun.

Wrong: Ce sont de musiciens connus.

Correct: Ce sont des musiciens connus.

2) Using indirect object pronouns

Indirect object pronouns are placed before the verb.

WrongCorrect

Tu veux présenter ton professeur à je?


Tu veux me présenter ton professeur ?

Do you want to introduce your teacher to me?

Mon petit ami veut offrir un cadeau à tu.

Mon petit ami veut t’offrir un cadeau.

My boyfriend wants to give me a present.

Je vais montrer mon amour à il.


Je vais lui montrer mon amour.

I want to show him my love.

Le professeur pose une question à nous.


Le professeur nous pose une question.

The teacher asks us a question.

Je veux demander un petite chose à vous.


Je veux vous demander une petite chose.

I want to show you a small thing.

Je dis "oui" à ils.


Je leur dis "oui".

I say "yes" to them.

Attention:

a) The indirect and direct object pronouns for the first person singular/plural and second person singular/plural look identical.

For example:

  • Je te regarde. ("te", direct object pronoun)

I look at you.

  • Je te réponds. ("te", indirect object pronoun, "répondre à" is a complete phrase, and "à" is no longer needed with indirect object pronouns)

I reply you.

b) "Penser à" (to miss/think of/about) is an exception where the object pronoun after "à" can only be a direct one.

Wrong : Je te pense.

Correct : Je pense à toi.

I miss you.

201200.Value:
"Comme" (because/as/since)

"Comme" can lead a complete statement to give reasons for something. "Comme" is placed at the very beginning of a sentence.

Example

Comme je suis Français, je sais parler français.

Since I'm French, I know how to speak French. 

Comme il fait beau, nous pouvons aller au parc.

Since the weather is nice, we can go to the park.

201241.Value:
Relative Pronoun "Qui"

"Qui" (who/that) can refer to either people or objects. 

In a relative clause where "qui" is the introductory word, "qui" acts as the subject. 

Its form will never change, but, same as in English, the verb in the relative clause must be in the appropriate form that agrees in both gender and number with the subject in the main clause.

Example

Adrien est un musicien qui parle français.

Adrien is a musician who speaks French.

Les filles qui regardent papa sont mes soeurs.

The girls who are looking at dad are my sisters.

C’est nous qui voulons acheter ces chaussures.

It's us who want to buy these shoes.

201247.Value:
Relative Pronoun "Que"

In a relative clause introduced by "que" (that), it functions as the direct object, which can be either people or objects.

Example

C’est le stylo que je veux acheter.

This is the pen that I want to buy.

La question que tu me pose est difficile.

The question that you raised to me is difficult.

Les cadeaux que je t’offre sont chers.

The gift that I give you are expensive.

201253.Value:
Relative Pronoun "Où" and "D’où"

"Où" (where) can refer to only places or time.

Example

C’est la ville où j’habite.

This is the city where I live.

C’est l’école où elle fait ses études.

This is the school where she studies.

C’est le bureau où je travaille.

This is the office where I work.

When the noun "où" points to is preceded by the preposition "de", we must keep "de". Then the relative pronoun would become "d’où".

Example

C’est la ville d’où je viens.

This is the city where I come from.

C’est l’école d’où elle vient.

This is the school where she comes from.

201326.Value:
Relative Pronoun "Dont"

This one refers to possession, resembling the English "whose/of which". It is used to replace "de + person/object".

Without Relative Clause"Dont" Relative Clause

Je veux louer un studio, le prix du studio est bon marché.

I wan to rent a studio. Its price is cheap.

Je veux louer un studio dont le prix est bon marché.

I want to rent a studio whose price is cheap.

J’aime le salon, les fenêtres du salon sont grandes.

I like the living room. Its windows are large.

J’aime le salon dont les fenêtres sont grandes.

I like the living room whose windows are large.

C’est une ville, le paysage de la ville est très beau.

This is a city. Its views are very beautiful.

C’est une ville dont le paysage est très beau.

This is a city whose views are very beautiful.

201337.Value:
"Si" in Negation

"Si" and "non" both express negation.

Their difference is, "non" negates an affirmative statement whereas "si" confirms a negative statement.

QuestionReply

Es-tu Français ?

Are you French?

Non, je ne suis pas Français.

No, I'm not French.

Tu n’es pas Français ?

Are you not French?

Non, je ne suis pas Français.

No, I'm not French

Si, je suis Français.

Yes, I'm French.

Est-elle policière ?

Is she a police?

Non, elle n’est pas policière.

No, she's not a police.

Elle n’est pas policière ?

Is she not a police?

Non, elle n’est pas policière.

No, she's not a police.

Si, elle est policière.

Yes, she's a police.

201395.Value:
Reflexive Pronouns

Pronominal verbs are verbs that require reflexive pronouns on the side in order to make the meaning complete.

The subject and the object of this kind of verbs are the same.

The infinitive form of a pronominal verb must include the reflexive pronoun as well: 

reflexive pronoun + verb infinitive

Using gerunds

As said in the beginning, the gerund is used on two concurrent actions.

Example

Ce chanteur chante en dansant.

The male singer sings while dancing.

Je fais des devoirs en écoutant de la musique.

I do homework while listening to music.

Nous travaillons en attendant les vacances.

We work while waiting for the holiday.

The Present Conditional

We use the conditional to talk about something that may or may not occur. The conditional can also express a possibility, a wish or a suggestion.

How is it formed?

The present conditional verb form = the simple future verb stem + the imperfect verb ending

Simple Future

201497.Value:
Adverbial Pronoun "En"

There are two adverbial pronouns in French, "en" is one of them.

The purpose of using adverbial pronouns is to tidy up the sentence, avoiding unnecessary repetition.

Generally, "en" is placed before the verb.

The following is "en" in use:

1) Number + n. : "en" replaces the noun

QuestionCombien de bonbons as-tu ?

How many candies do you have?

Answer

J’en ai trois. （en=trois bonbons）

 I have three.

Il en a beaucoup.（en=beaucoup de bonbons）

He has a lot.

Attention: The number must remain.

2) "En" replaces indefinite articles/partitive articles

QuestionAs-tu du vin ?

Do you have wine? 

Answer

J’en ai.（en=du vin）

I have.

3) "En" replaces indirect objects introduced by "de"

QuestionParlez-vous de la cuisine chinoise ?

Are you talking about the Chinese cuisine?

Answer

Oui, nous en parlons.（en=parlons de la cuisine chinoise）

Yes, we are.

4) "En" replaces the "de + X" part in "adj. + de + X"

Example
La cuisine de ce restaurant est très bonne, nous en sommes satisfaits.
(en=satisfaits de la cuisine)

The food of this restaurant is very good. We are very satisfied with it.

201530.Value:
Neuter Pronoun "Le"

"Le" can take the place of one word or an entire object clause.

Example

Vous voulez un médecin ? Je le suis.

(le= médecin)

Do YOU want to be a doctor? Yes, I do.

Vous êtes contents, nous le sommes aussi.

(le=contents)

You are happy. So are we.


Example

Il vient demain ?! Je ne le sais pas !

(le=Je ne sais pas s’il vient.)

He's coming tomorrow?! I don't know that!

Quand il est venu ? Dis-le-moi !

(le=Dis-moi quand il est venu.)

When did he come? Tell me that!

201741.Value:
Demonstrative Pronouns

Demonstrative pronouns are used to refer to nouns that have appeared previously in the context.

For starters, we are learning the simple demonstrative pronouns, which are often used in combination with "de".


m.f.

sg.

celui

(the one)

celle

(the one)

pl.

ceux

(the ones)

celles

(the ones)


We will see how they are used in these examples:

Example

Mon téléphone est rouge, celui de Matthieu est noir.

(celui = le téléphone de Matthieu)

My phone is red. The one of Matthieu/Matthieu's is black.

La carte de ce restaurant est plus jolie que celle de votre restaurant.

(celle =la carte de votre restaurant)

The menu of this restaurant is prettier that the one of your(pl.) restaurant.

Mes cheveux sont blonds, ceux d’Adrein est bruns.

(ceux=les cheveux d’Adrien)

My hair is blonde. Adrien's is brown.

Vos robes sont petites, celles de nos amies sont grandes.

(celles = les robes de nos amies)

Your(pl.) dresses are small. The ones of our friends are large.

201765.Value:
Present Subjunctive

Subjunctives express the speaker’s subjective judgment/opinions.

The subjunctive always appears after "que (that)". 

A present subjunctive verb form = the stem of the present ils/elles-verb conjugation + the following subjunctive endings:

Infinitive
ils/elles
conjugation
Present Subjunctive

vivre

to live

ils/elles vivent

que je vive

que tu vives

qu'il vive

que nous vivions

que vous viviez

qu'ils vivent

201789.Value:
Expletive "Ne"

The expletive "ne" does not have any actual meaning. 

It needs to be added to a subjunctive clause when we want to express negative emotions such as doubt, disappointment or fear.

Example

J’ai peur que tu ne reviennes pas.

I'm afriad that you wouldn't come back.

J’ai peur que tu ne reviennes.

I'm afraid that you would come back.

Elle est déçue que vous ne mentissiez.

She's disappointed that you(pl.) would lie.

201813.Value:
The subjunctive is necessary when making an impersonal statement starting with "il faut.../it vaut mieux". 

For example:

Il faut que tu travailles. (You should work. / lit. trans: It's necessary that you work.)

201816.Value:
The subjunctive is necessary when making an impersonal statement starting with "il faut.../it vaut mieux". 

For example:

Il vaut mieux que vous passiez cet examen. (You’d better pass this exam./ lit. trans: it's better that you pass this exam.)

201858.Value:
Inversion with "-"

Inverting the subject pronoun and the verb in a question requires the hyphen "-".

This question structure is only applicable to sentences where the subject is a pronoun. 

Use the "est-ce que" structure when the subject is a noun or noun phrase.

201864.Value:
A regular plural noun is built by adding "-s" to its singular form. Be aware that some nouns are irregular, and some only exist in plural.

Apparently, a plural noun would require a plural indefinite article:

SingularPlural

un homme

a man

des hommes

some men

un melon

a melon

des melons

some melons

201875.Value:
Nationality

In French, nationality nouns and adjectives share the same spelling. There’re similar cases in English: "Italian/Japanese" (noun) and "Italian/Japanese" (adjective).

The only difference between a nationality noun and an adjective in French is, the noun is capitalized whereas the adjective isn’t.

For example:

Français (Frenchman) - C’est un Français. (This is a Frenchman.)

français (French) - Il est français. (He is French.)

Attention:

In practice, when the subject is a pronoun, French people use the adjective to talk about nationality, as in "I/you/he/she/we/you/they is/are + adj.".

Wrong: Elle est une Française. (She is a French woman.)

Correct: Elle est française. (She is French.)

  1. Noun Genders
  2. The Indefinite and Definite Articles (Singular)
  3. Subject Pronouns 1
  4. Verb Conjugation: "Être" (to be)

Extended Reading

  1. Nationalities
  2. H aspiré & H muet
  3. Elision


1. Noun Genders

All nouns in French are either masculine or feminine whether it refers to an animate or inanimate object.

This means grammatical elements such as articles and adjectives must align with the gender of the noun they pair with. 

Generally, a masculine noun is signified by "m." and a feminine one by "f.".


Noun
Article + Noun

m.

homme

man

un homme

a man

f.

femme

woman

une femme

a woman


2. The Indefinite and Definite Articles (Singular)

The indefinite and definite articles each have three forms: masculine singular, feminine singular and plural.

Let's look at the singular article here:

Article
For a Singular Noun
m.
f.

Indefinite

un

a

une

a

Definite

le OR l’

the

la OR l’

the

Attention: When a definite article precedes a singular noun that starts with a vowel (a, e, i, o, u, y ) or the mute "h", "le/la" becomes "l’" for the convenience of pronunciation.

For example, "l’homme" and "l’enfant".


3. Subject Pronouns 1

In total, French has eight subject pronouns.

We will first introduce the singular ones:

Person
Singular

First

je

I

Second

tu

you

Third

il/elle

he/she/it


Attention: When it comes to objects, unlike the all-inclusive "it" in English, we choose "il" or "elle" according to the gender of the noun. For example:

English: I eat a lemon. It is sour.

You eat an apple. It is sour.

French: Je mange un citron. Il est acide. ("Citron" is masculine)

Tu manges une pomme. Elle est acide. ("Pomme" is feminine)


4. Verb Conjugation: "Être" (to be)

Verb conjugation exists in many languages, and French is one of those.

The original form of a verb is called an infinitive. Conjugation occurs when an infinitive changes its form to agree with the sentence subject, tense, mood etc.

Pronoun
Être
Example

je

I

suis

Je suis un garçon.

I am a boy.

tu

you

es

Tu es une fille.

You are a girl.

il

he

est

Il est français.

He is French.

elle

she

est

Elle est française.

She is French.



Extended Reading:

1. Nationality

In French, nationality nouns and adjectives share the same spelling. The only difference between a nationality noun and an adjective in French is, the noun has the first letter capitalized whereas the adjective doesn't. 

For example:

Français (Frenchman) - C’est un Français. (This is a Frenchman.)

français (French) - Il est français. (He is French.)

Attention:

In practice, when the subject is a pronoun, French people use the adjective to talk about nationality, as in "I/you/he/she/we/you/they is/are + adj.".

Wrong: Elle est une Française. (She is a French woman.)

Correct: Elle est française. (She is French.)


2. H aspiré & H muet

There are two types of "h"s in French: H aspiré (the aspirated H) and H muet (the mute H). Regardless, neither of them is pronounced. In other words, the French consonant "h" is always silent.

  • Words starting with H aspiré do not have elision with the word before them (see 3. for the detailed explanation of "elision");
  • Words starting with H muet must contract with the word before them.

Unfortunately, there isn't a one-size-fits-all rule for distinguishing H aspiré and H muet. You will have to remember them case by case.

In most cases, H aspiré appears before "au" or "ou": e.g. "le haut", "la hanche" and "la houille", while H muet appears before other vowels: e.g. "l'habitude", "l'homme" and "l’huile".


3. Elision

The term "elision", sometimes also called "contraction", happens when a vowel-ending word drops the vowel when it is followed by a vowel-initial word, and the ending vowel is replaced by an apostrophe. 

In French, elision occurs when some one-syllable words (such as articles) precede a noun or a verb starting with a vowel or H muet, thus creating a sliding effect in pronunciation.

For example:

Before Elision
After Elision

la + enfant

l’enfant

le + homme

l’homme

ne + est

n’est

si + il

s’il

3000002.Value:
Learning Tips for Food

  1. Partitive Articles
  2. Verb Conjugation: "boire" (to drink)


1. Partitive Articles

A. What is a partitive article?

Apart from the definite and indefinite articles, French uses partitive articles for uncountable nouns, such as liquid or food.

A partitive article consists of the preposition "de" and a definite article:

"the" nounde + "the" + nounExample

le pain

the bread

du (de le) pain

some bread

Je mange du pain.

I'm eating (some) bread.

le café

the coffee

du (de le) café

some coffee

Tu bois du café.

You are drinking (some) coffee.

B. When do we use a partitive article?

As said, partitive articles are used for unspecified quantity or uncountable concepts.

Most foods and beverages are uncountable. Hence, partitive articles are suitable for these nouns.

Partitive ArticleGenderExample
du
m.

du vin

some wine

de laf.

de la viande

some meat

de l'

(The definite article after "de" follows the elision rule.)
m. & f.

de l’agneau (m.)

some lamb

de l’eau (f.)

some water


2. Verb Conjugation in the Present: "Boire" (to drink)

elle

3000003.Value:
Learning Tips for Questions 1

Part I

  1. Yes-No Questions 1
  2. Yes-No Questions 2 
  3. "-t-" 

Part II

  1. Negation
  2. Using "de" in Negation
  3. Using definte articles for generic references
     

Part I

1. Yes-Or-No Questions (informal)

The easiest way to form an informal yes-no question in French is by adding the fixed expression "est-ce que" in front of a statement. "Est-ce que" is literally "is it that". 

"Est-ce que  + statement ?"


StatementYes-no Question

Tu es professeur.

You are a teacher.

Est-ce que tu es professeur ?

Are you a teacher?

(Is it that you're a teacher?)

Il est avocat.

He is a lawyer.

Est-ce qu’il est avocat ?

Is he a lawyer?

(Is it that he's a lawyer?)

Chloe boit du thé.

She is drinking some tea.

Est-ce que Chloe boit du thé ?

Is Chloe drinking some tea?

(Is it that Chloe is drinking some tea?)

Attention:

① Keep in mind that elision is mandatory when the subject after "que" starts with a vowel or H muet as seen in the second example above.
② In French, there is no need to put an indefinite article before a profession when talking about someone’s job.


2. Yes-No Questions (formal)

The formal way to form a yes-no question is by using the inversion and the right subject pronoun : 

Verb-Subject Pronoun + Object ? 

StatementYes-no Question

Tu es professeur.

You are a teacher.

Es-tu professeur ?

Are you a teacher?

Il est avocat.

He is a lawyer.

Est-il avocat ?

Is he a lawyer?

Elle boit du thé.

She's drinking some tea.

Boit-elle du thé ?

Is she drinking some tea?

Attention:

1. In French, when inversion happens, a hyphen is required to link the verb and the subject.

2. We may use this construction only when the subject is a subject pronoun (like "tu/il/elle"). 


3. "-t-" 

In a "verb-il/elle(s)" construction, if the verb ends with a vowel, we use "-t-" instead of "-" to link the two elements, so the sentence sounds more phonetically comfortable.

For example: "Aime-t-il les croissants ?" (Does he like croissants?)

vowel-ending verb + il/elle(s)-t-

aime + il

aime-t-il

aime + elle

aime-t-elle

va + il

va-t-il


4. Using definite articles for generic references

The definite articles "le/la/les/l'" have another use besides specifying objects. Quite paradoxically, definite articles can refer to a class or group of things in some situations. 

For example, "I like apples" in French is "j'aime les pommes", not "j'aime des pommes", because the action "aimer (to like)" usually deals with a whole group. The definite article "les" refers to apples as a type of fruit, not certain apples. 

If the object being liked is uncountable, e.g. "le lait (the milk"), it's "J'aime le lait (I like milk)."

Likewise, when we negate "aimer (to like)", we also negate the whole group and use the definite articles in negation. 


Part II

1. Negation

Here is how to negate a verb in French: 

"ne + verb + pas"

However, if the negated verb has a vowel or H muet as the first letter, "ne" becomes "n’" and contracts with the following word.

Examples:

AffirmativeNegative

Tu es professeur.

You are a teacher.

Tu n'es pas professeur.

You're not a teacher.

Il est avocat.

He is a lawyer.

Il n'est pas avocat.

He's not a lawyer.



2. Using "de" in Negation

To negate actions that involve objects, we can use "de". You can think of it as the word "any" in English. Pay attention to the number of the noun in the negative statements.


QuestionsNegative Statements
Uncountable Object

Bois-tu du soda.

Are you drinking some soda?

Je ne bois pas de soda.

I don’t drink/am not drinking any soda.

Countable Object

Manges-tu des pommes?.

Are you eating (some) apples .

Je ne mange pas de pommes.

I'm not eating any apples.

3000004.Value:
Learning Tips for Questions 2

Part I

  1. Qui- and Que-questions with inversion
  2. Qui- and Que-questions with "est-ce que"

Part II

  1. "Ce" as Demonstrative Pronouns
  2. Preposition "de" 


Part I

1. Qui and Que

When "qui (who)" serves as the subject of the verb in the question, the word order is identical to that of a statement: 

"Qui + verb + object?"

Example

Qui mange du fromage ?

Who eats cheese?


When "que (what)" and "qui (who)" are the sentence object and when the subject is a pronoun, we can form the question with "-" and inversion:

"Que/qui + verb-pronoun?"

Example

Que bois-tu ?

What are you drinking?

Qui es-tu ?

What are you?

Que mange-t-elle ?

What does she eat?


2. Que/Qui with "est-ce que"

When "que/qui" are the sentence objects, we can also form que/qui-questions with "est-ce que", which we learned in the unit about yes-no questions. The construction is very simple, just follow these 3 steps:

① Take a statement, e.g. "Adrien mange une pomme", and replace the sentence object with "que": Adrien mange + que ? 

② Move "que" to the beginning of the sentence and then add "est-ce que" behind it: Que + est-ce que + Adrien mange ?

③ Contract words according to the rule of elision: Qu'est-ce qu'Adrien mange ? (It's literally "what is it that Adrien eats?")

 More examples: 

Statements
with inversionwith "est-ce que"
Elle boit du vin.
She's drinking some wine.		Que boit-elle ?
What does she drink?		Qu'est-ce qu'elle boit ?
What is it that she drinks?
What is she drinking?
Louis aime Chloe.
Louis loves Chloe. 		Qui Louis aime-t-il ?*
 Who does Louis love?
Qui est-ce que Louis aime ?
Who is it that Louis loves?
Who does Louis love?

Attention:
1. Qui cannot contract with "est-ce que".
2. *This construction is the most formal and extended form, yet it's rarely used in daily conversations. Beginners are not required to master this form. 

 

Part II

1. "Ce" as Pronouns

The demonstrative pronoun "ce" can refer to either an animate being or an inanimate object. It corresponds to "this/that/these/those", all of them, in English.

"Ce" has only one form when it is a pronoun, regardless of the number/gender of the noun it replaces. 

Let's take a look at the sentences with "ce" as the subject:

Example

C’est un livre.

This is a book.

Ce n’est pas une fille.

This is not a girl.

Ce sont des cahiers.

These are some notebooks.

Attention:
When the verb after "ce" starts with a vowel, elision takes place and "ce" is contracted to c’.


2. Preposition "de"

"de" has many uses, and one of them is to indicate possession and ownership.

"A de B" = A of/belongs to B

In writing, "de" follows the rule of elision.

Example

le livre d’Adrien

Adrien’s book/the book of Adrien

la pomme d’une fille

a girl’s apple/the apple of a girl

les écouteurs de ce garçon

the headphones of this boy


Contracting "de" with definite articles

The definite articles "le" and "les" contract with "de" and some prepositions, which we will learn later. 

The following chart shows us a summary of the contracted forms of "de" with the definite articles:


de + Definite ArticlesContractionExample

Contracted

de + le

du

le livre du professeur

the book of the teacher’s / the teacher’s book

de + les

des

les cahiers des étudiants

the notebooks of the students’ / the students’ notebooks

Uncontracted

de + la

de la

la clé de la fille

the key of the girl’s / the girl’s key

3. The Plural Definite Articles and Nouns

The easiest way to make a singular noun plural is by adding "-s" to the singular noun. But some nouns don't follow this pattern, and some are always used in the plural form. We will talk about them when we encounter them in the future.

The plural definite article for both masculine and feminine groups is "les". 

Definite Article (pl.)

les Français

the French people

les Américains

the Americans

les femmes

the women

When a plural concept includes both masculine and feminine members, the plural noun takes the masculine form. For example, a group of French people with both men and women is referred to as "les Français".


4. Identifying Noun Genders 

1) Some nouns for peoples or professions have two forms, one for male and one for female.

The most common way to change a masculine noun to a feminine one is by adding "-e" to the end of the masculine noun. 

For example:

  • "étudiant" (male college student) + e => "étudiante" (female college student)
  • Français (Frenchman) + e => Française (Frenchwoman)

2) Some nouns share the same form in both genders. 

One example is "professeur (male/female teacher)". For these nouns, we can rely on the article to differentiate the gender. For example, "un professeur" tells us it is a male teacher and "une professeur" a female one.

3) For "-en"-ending nouns, attaching "-ne" at the end gives us the feminine form:

Masculine
Feminine

musicien

male musician

musicienne

female musician

Italien

Italian man

Italienne

Italian woman

lycéen

male high school student

lycéenne

female high school student

2. What’s Your Name?

Note that the verb "s'appeler" literally means "call oneself  ...". It's the most common way to introduce one's name. 

For this level of learning, don't worry about the conjugation rules. Just memorize them like a fixed expression.

Question

4. French Surnames with "de"

In the old days, a surname with "de" would tell people that you are from a noble family. Nowadays, a surname with "de" still implies the possibility of a noble root, but it is not a guarantee anymore.

Learning Tips for Objects

  1. "Ce" as Demonstrative Adjectives
  2. Using Plural Nouns


1. Demonstrative Adjectives & Pronouns "Ce"

A. "Ce" as Adjectives

Demonstrative adjectives are words like "this ...", "that ...", "those ..." They agree with the noun they refer to in both gender and number. Therefore, there is more than one form of demonstrative adjectives in French.

"Ce" can mean "this" as well as "that". To distinguish, "-là" is attached to the noun to indicate "that".

sg.
m.
ce 

this
ce livre

this book

ce ...-là

that

ce portable-là

that cell phone

cet

this

cet ordinateur

this computer

cet ...-là

that

cet enfant-là

that child

f.

cette

this

cette ...-là

that

pl.

ces ...-là

Attention:
When the demonstrative adjective for masculine singular nouns "ce" appears before a noun starting with a vowel or H muet, for the convenience of pronunciation, "ce" takes the form "cet":

- cet ordinateur (this computer)

- cet enfant (this child)

B. Pronoun vs. Adjective

Demonstrative Pronoun
Demonstrative Adjective

C’est un melon.

Ce melon est vert.

Ce sont des garçons.

These boys are French.



2. Using Plural Nouns

A certain number of French nouns are always used in the plural form.

For example:

n. (sg.)

!! To describe "hair, beard", we use the color "roux" instead. It's not the same color as "rouge", which is usually reserved for pure red objects.

2. Adjective-noun agreement:

First of all, let’s clarify two terms: attributive and predicative adjectives.

  • An attributive adjective goes before the noun it describes, as in "a green apple".
  • A predicative adjective follows a linking verb, as in "the apple is green".

Now, there are two principles to bear in mind about French adjectives:

  1. Adjectives always agree in both gender and number with the noun they modify, regardless of positions.
  2. An attributive color adjective is placed AFTER the modified noun. 

Here are a few examples:

Example

un melon vert

a green melon

une pomme verte

a green apple

les cheveux noirs

black hair (lit. black hairs)

des téléphones noires

some black telephones

Attention: 
Adjectives ending in "-e" remain the same both masculine and feminine nouns. 

For example:

Example

un portable rouge

a red cell phone

une pomme rouge

a red apple

Learning Tips for Animals

  1. Verb Conjugation: "Avoir" (to have)
  2. Using "de" in Negation 2
  3. Sensations


1. Verb Conjugation in the Present: "Avoir" (to have)

Subject Pronoun (sg.)
Avoir
Example

je

ai

J’ai trois chats.

I have three cats.

tu

as

Tu as quatre chiens.

You have four dogs.

il/elle

a

Il a deux moutons.

He has two sheep.

nous

avons

Nous avons dix poissons.

We have ten fish.

vous

Vous avez un tigre.

ils/ elles

ont

Les étudiants ont cinq professeurs.

The students have five teachers.


2. Singular or Plural in Absolute Negation

Let’s refresh our memory on using "de" in negation.

"de" is similar to "any". It replaces the indefinite article or the partitive article before the noun.

Example

Je n’ai pas de chats.

I don’t have any cats.

Elles n’ont pas de tigres.

They (female) don’t have any tigers.

Valérie n’a pas d’enfants.

Valérie doesn’t have any children.


Attention: 
If we are answering a question that questions a singular noun, we use "de + singular noun".

Example

- As-tu un loup ?

- Non, je n’ai pas de loup.

- Do you have a wolf?

- No, I don’t have a wolf.


3. Sensations

To express sensations, we use the fixed expression, "avoir + feeling (noun)".

The noun is in the singular form and follows "avoir" directly.

Example

J’ai chaud.

I’m hot.

 (as a result of the air temperature)

Ce loup a faim.

This wolf is hungry.

Nous avons soif.

We’re thirsty.

2. The Position of Attributive Adjectives

These adjectives can be placed BEFORE the modified noun if they:

  • consist of one or two syllables;
  • are descriptive adjectives
  • are not colors
Example

These adjectives are placed AFTER the modified noun if they:

  • consist of more than 2 syllables
  • are colors

Relatively further away from the speaker, a vague reference; colloquially though, "là" is often used interchangeably with "ici". To avoid causing confusion for beginners, we are not including this usage in this unit.

là-bas

over there

Far from the speaker, an exact location

Examples :

Location Adverb
Example

ici

Je suis ici.

I am here

This is a park here.

là

Tu es là.

You are there.

là-bas

That is a bar over there.

I like that café over there, I drink coffee there.


2. Preposition "à"

No article is needed before a city name in the expression "in ___ (insert city name)".

Example

à Lyon

in Lyon

Je suis à Paris.

I’m in Paris.

Les médecins sont à Nice.

The doctors are in Nice.

Part I

1. Verb Groups and "-er" Ending Verbs

The dictionary form of a verb is called the infinitive, and it consists of a stem and an ending. In French, a verb changes with the sentence subject, tense, mood etc. For each subject pronoun, a verb has a corresponding conjugated form.

French verbs fall into three groups depending on the ending: Verbs ending in "-er", verbs ending in "-ir" and all other verbs.

In this unit, we are talking about the conjugation rules of the first verb group, verbs ending in "-er".  (Some "-er" ending verbs change both the stem and the ending in the conjugated forms, but we will leave them out for now.)

2. Using "Parler" (to speak) and "Étudier" (to study)

1) The expression "étudier + definite article + language" means to make an effort in acquiring a language.

Example

to study Arabic

2) To say "to speak a language", we use "parler + language" without articles.

Example

parler arabe

to speak Arabic

We speak Italian.

Adrien parle français, espagnol et anglais.

Adrien speaks French, Spanish and English.


3. Languages and Nationalities

In French, a nationality and its corresponding language are spelled the same, but the nationality noun has its first letter capitalized.

Languages are always masculine in French.

4. Sur + Website/App

To say we are on a certain website/app, we put the preposition "sur" before the website/app name:

Example

Part II

1. Adverb "aussi" 

The adverb "aussi" means "too/also/as well" and follows the verb.

Example

Il a aussi un chat.

He has a cat, too.

Mon professeur est aussi M. Aragon.

My teacher is also Mr. Aragon.

1. Countries in French

French country names have grammatical genders and require definite articles in most cases. Most country names ending with "e" are feminine, and the rest are masculine. Only a few country names never appear with articles. 

les Pays - the Countries

masculine

le Japon, le Mexique, le Portugal, le Brésil

feminine

la France, l'Angleterre, l'Italie, l'Espagne, la Chine, la Corée du Sud, la Russie

plural

les Etats-Unis, les Pays-Bas, les Philippines


2. The Third Verb Group

This group is the irregular group, which means we will need to memorize them one by one. For example,"être" (to be) and "avoir" (to have) belong to this group.

3. Verb Conjugation in the Present: "Venir" (to come)

Subject PronounVenirExample

je

Je viens d’Angleterre.

I come from the UK.

viens

He comes from Rome.

nous

Nous venons de France.

We come from France.

vous

venez

Vous venez des Etats-Unis.

You come from the US.

ils/ elles

viennent

Les garçons viennent du Mexique.

The boys come from Mexico.


4. Preposition "de" 3

"de" can also suggest the origin or source of something.

When "venir" and "de" are employed to express "to come from a place", the location noun may or may not require a definite aritlce. We will explain when to add an article and when not to.

1) To come from a city, no article:

CityExample

Paris

Je viens de Paris.

I come from Paris.

Toulouse

Adrien vient de Toulouse.

Adrien comes from Toulouse.

2) To come from a country whose proper noun is feminine, no article:

CountryExample

la France

Nous venons de France.

We come from France.

l’Italie

Les filles viennent d’Italie.

The girls come from Italy.


3) To come from a country whose proper noun is masculine, a definite article is mandatory, so use the correct "de+le/les" contractions:

CountryExample

le Mexique

Elle ne vient pas du Mexique.

She doesn’t come from Mexico.

le Portugal

Est-ce que tu viens du Portugal ?

Do you come from Portugal?

(les) Etats-Unis

Les étudiants viennent des Etats-Unis.

The students come from the US.

(les) Pays-Bas

Venez-vous des Pays-Bas ?

Do you come from the Netherlands?


5. Verb Conjugation in the Present Tense: "Boire" (to drink)

In the second unit "Food", we had the conjugations of "boire" for singular subject pronouns. Here we will look at its conjugations for "nous", "vous" and "ils/ells".

vous

buvez

You are drinking (some) milk.

ils/elles

boivent

They are drinking (some) coffee.

Learning Tips for Location 2

  1. "À" + definite articles
  2. "In a Country"
  3. Question Words "Où" & "D’où"


1. "À" + definite articles

In the previous lessons, we learned the contracted forms of "de" plus definite articles. Now we will take a look at how "à" is contracted with the definite articles.


à + Def. Art.
Contraction
Example


Contracted

à + le

au

Je suis au bar.

I’m at the bar.

à + les

aux

Ils sont aux Etats-Unis.

They are in the US.


Not Contracted

à + la

à la

Nous sommes à la banque.

We’re in the bank.

à + l’

à l’

Les médecins sont à l’hôpital.

The doctors are in the hospital.


2. "In a Country"

To say "in a specific country", the use of prepositions is a bit more complex in French, because country names have grammatical genders.

1) When the country name is a feminine noun, use "en":

Example

Nous sommes en France.

We are in France.

Es-tu en Allemagne?

Are you in Germany?

2) When the country name is a masculine singular noun, use "au":

Example

Il ne travaille pas au Portugal.

He doesn’t work in Portugal.

Elle n’habite pas au Mexique.

She doesn’t live in Mexico.

3) When the country name is a plural noun, use "aux":

Example

Les musiciens sont aux Etats-Unis.

The musicians are in the US.

Cet homme travaille aux Pays-Bas.

This man works in the Netherlands.


3. Question Words "Où" & "D’où"

Adverbial question words are "où (where)", "quand (where)", "comment (how)", "combien (how much)". They refer to the adverbs of location or time, etc. in a statement. A simple question with adverbial question words in French follows this structure:

Adverbial question word + verb + subject  ?


1) when the subject is a pronoun, don't forget the hyphen "-":

Statement
Question

Nous habitons à Paris.

We live in Paris.

Où habitons-nous ?

Where do we live?

Elle travaille au supermarché.

She works in the supermarket.

Où travaille-t-elle ?

Where does she work?


2) To ask where someone comes from, we use the question word "d’où", which is made up of "de + où". The preposition "de" can’t be left out. When the subject is a pronoun, we can say: "d'où verb-pronoun ?"

Statement
Question

Ces hommes viennent des Etats-Unis.

These men come from the US.

D’où viennent ces hommes?

Where do these men come from?

1. Direct Object Pronouns

Verbs can take direct or indirect objects, which can be replaced by pronouns to avoid repetitiveness. 

In French, direct object pronouns come BEFORE verbs. For example, instead of "I love you", it would be "I you love" in French.

As always, taking the rule of elision into account, if the verb behind the pronoun starts with a vowel or H muet, they need to be contracted.

 Using direct object pronouns in sentences:

Example

We like French, we study it.

Cette fille est très jolie, les garçons la regardent.

This girl is pretty, the boys look at her.

Elle nous attend.

She waits for us.

Valérie vous regarde.

Valérie looks at you.

Ce sont des musiciens, je les aime bien.

They are musicians, I like them very much.

3. Aimer & Aimer Bien

When we use "aimer" alone, it means "to love".

However, when we use the adverb "bien" after "aimer", "aimer bien" simply means "to like".

Example

J’aime Pierre

I love Pierre.

J’aime bien ce professeur.

I like this teacher.

Learning Tips for Week

  1. Weekdays
  2. What Day Is It Today?
  3. "Ce" day vs. "Le" day
  4. Verb Conjugation in the Present Tense: "Aller" (to go)


1. Weekdays

Weekday

lundi

Monday

Tuesday

mercredi

Wednesday

Thursday

Sunday

Days of the week are not capitalized in French!


2. What Day Is It Today?

When asking and answering what day of the week it is, we use the question word "quel" (which), not the "que (what)". The expression is literally "which day are we (on)"?

Example

Quel jour sommes-nous ?

What day is it today?

Nous sommes lundi.

It’s Monday today.

Quel jour sommes-nous aujourd’hui?

What day is it today?

Nous sommes vendredi aujourd’hui.

It’s Friday today.


3. "Ce" day vs. "Le" day

"Ce" is always the more definite determiner, same as "this". "Le" plus a weekday means "all/every (weekday)". Remember, "le/la + singular noun" can represent the general concept of something.

Example

le lundi

every Monday

Je suis au bar le vendredi.

I’m at the bar every Friday.

ce mercredi

this Wednesday

Je vais à l’école ce mardi.

I go to school this Tuesday.


4. Verb Conjugation in the Present Tense: "Aller" (to go)

The verb "aller" is an irregular one. To say "go to somewhere", we need the preposition "à" after "aller".

aller
je vais
tu vas
il/elle va
nous allons
vous allez
ils/elles vont

Learning Tips for Time

  1. Impersonal Subject "Il"
  2. Telling Time


1. Impersonal Subject "Il"

We already know "il" as the subject pronoun "he".

Besides that, "il" can be used as the impersonal subject, similar to the English "it".

Example

Il est midi.

It’s noon now.

Il est une heure et demie.

It’s one thirty now.

Il fait froid.

It’s cold.


2. Questions about Time

1) To ask "What time is it?" we need the feminine form of "quel" -- "quelle", for "heure (hour)" is feminine. The structure is,

Quelle heure est-il ?

To ask when something happens, we need the question word "quand (when)". The structure is,

Quand + verb + subject ?

or when the subject is a pronoun: Quand + verb-pronoun ?


2) Telling time:

Il est + X heure(s)

Example

Quelle heure est-il ?

What time is it?

Il est trois heures.

It’s three o’clock.

Quelle heure est-il maintenant?

What time is it?

Il est minuit.

It’s midnight.

Attention:

a) To say "6 in the morning", for example, it's "six heures du matin", where "du" is the contraction of "de" and "le".

b) When "demi" (half) follows a noun, it must agree in gender with the noun. For example, "une heure et demie" (an hour and a half).


5) In the unit of "Weekdays", we mentioned that the definite article "le" expresses the idea of "every/each", and the demonstrative adjective "ce" means "this". This rule can be applied to the time of day as well.

Example

le midi

each noon

cet après-midi

6) Doing something at a specific time:

... + à + specific time

(Attention: "midi" and "minuit" are considered specific times in French.)

Example

I come at 13 o’clock (1 PM).

Il ne travaille pas à minuit.

He doesn’t work at midnight.

Je vais à la banque à midi.

2) Exception: "grand" in "grand-mère" does not need adjective-noun gender agreement.


2. Possessive Adjective + Singular Noun

The rule of adjective-noun agreement applies to possessive adjectives, too. In this unit, we will familiarize ourselves with the singular form first.

Attention: 
The gender agreement rule can be overridden to avoid hiatus, which is the break between a vowel-ending word and vowel-initial word. If n.f. in "ma/ta/sa (+adj.) + n.f." starts with a vowel or H muet, we use "mon/ton/son (+adj.) + n.f." instead so that it sounds more better. For example: "your idea" should be "ton idée" instead of "ta idée". 


3. Interrogative Adjective "Quel" (which)

Beware that "quel" is an interrogative adjective, and it should be used with a noun.

Here are what we have learned about using "quel" so far:

Quel(le) + n. + v. + subject ?

Example

Quel jour sommes-nous ?

What day is it today? 

(lit. Which day are we?)

A French phone number consists of ten digits. It is customary in France to read a phone number by two digits. If the first digit is 0, 0 and the digit right after it will be stated separately first before reading the rest by two digits.

For example:

The reading of this phone number "06 42 35 16 08" is:

zéro six, quarante-deux, trente-cinq, seize, zéro huit

The question word (interrogative pronoun) "combien" means "how many/much".

Part I 

1. Asking for Price

The question word "combien" means "how much, how many". To ask for the cost of something, we can say:

Combien + coûter + noun ？

"Coûter" follows the conjugation rules for "-er" ending verbs.

Question
Answer

Combien coûte ce sac ?

How much does this bag cost?

Il coûte cinq euros.

It costs five euro.

Combien coûtent ces chaussures noires  ?

How much do these black shoes cost?

Elles coûtent trente dollars.

They cost thirty dollars.

Attention:

1) For exact multiples of the hundred (200, 500, etc.), "cent" needs "-s", as in "deux cents" and "trois cents". When it's not the exact multiples of 100, lose the "-s".

2) "mille" never adds "-s".


3. Impersonal Pronoun "Ça" (it/this)

1) "ça" is used colloquially to refer to a vague object or a group of objects. Do not use it on people or a specified object.

Learning Tips for Ordinal Numbers

Part I (Lesson 1)

  1. Interrogative Pronoun "Lequel"
  2. Ordinal Numbers

Part II (Lesson 2)

Part I

1. Interrogative Pronoun "Lequel"

"Lequel" means "which one(s)", and it must agree in both gender and number with the noun it refers to. 

We will start with the singular forms first:

"Lequel" for n. (m. sg.)

"Laquelle" for n. (f. sg.)

Example

Laquelle de ces filles est ta fille ?

Which one of these girls is your daughter?

Regardez ces hommes, lequel est ton mari ?

Look at these men, which one is your husband?

Il y a des stylos ici, lequel est mon stylo ?

There are some pens here, which one is my pen?

Attention:
"Lequel" can be used to replace "quel + noun".

Lequel
Quel + noun

Laquelle est ma chemise ?

Which one is my shirt?

Quelle chemise est ma chemise ?

Which shirt is my shirt?

Lequel est mon professeur ?

Which one is my teacher?

Quel homme est mon professeur ?

Which man is my teacher?


2. Ordinal Numbers

1) A few examples:

FR-EN

premier/première

first

deuxième/second(e)

second

troisième

third

quatrième

fourth

cinquième

fifth

sixième

sixth

septième

seventh

vingt et unième

twenty-first

trente et unième

thirty-first

centième

one hundredth

2) Constructing ordinal numbers

  • Cardinal numbers ending with a consonant: + -ième
  • Cardinal numbers ending with "-e": replace "-e" with "-ième"

Ordinal numbers written this way are both masculine and feminine. Except:

  • "1st" is "premier" for masculine nouns and "première" for feminine nouns.
  • "2nd" can be "deuxième" or "second(e)". But the French "second(e)" has two genders, masculine and feminine.
  • For numbers like "21st, 51st or 71st", the "-1st" is "et unième" instead of "premier".


3. School System

In France, middle school education lasts 4 years, and high school education lasts 3 years. To say "in X grade", we use the preposition "en".

FR
UK
US

la sixième

Year 7
6th grade

la cinquième

Year 8
7th grade

la quatrième

Year 9
8th grade

la troisième

Year 10
9th grade/freshman

la seconde

Year 11
10th grade/sophomore

la première

Year 12
11th grade/junior

la terminale

Year 13
12th grade/senior

Attention:
1) Always use the feminine form of the ordinal number when talking about school grades.
2) The second grade in French is always "la seconde", not "la deuxième". 


Part II 

1. Conjunction: "car" (because)

"Car" is used to connect the result and the cause. So both the consequence and the reason must be present in the sentence.

Example

Je ne t’aime pas, car je te déteste.

I don’t like you because I hate you.

Je travaille ici, car j’aime mon travail.

I work here because I like my job.


2. Floors in France

In Europe, the ground floor is called the "rez-de-chaussée (the ground floor)", not the first floor. The floor above the ground floor (2nd floor in other regions) is called the first floor.

3. Dates

Here is how to say dates in French:

2. Partitive Articles

In the unit "Food", we introduced partitive articles, which is used for food or liquid which has unspecified quantity.

In this unit, in the expression "écouter de la musique (to listen to music)", because "la musique" is a general concept and we can only listen to a musical piece, such as a song, we need to use the partitive article "de la".

Learning Tips for Present Progressive

  1. Talking about Hobbies
  2. Plural Nouns for Hobbies
  3. Present Progressive
  4. "Faire"-Questions: Present or Present Progressive?


1. Talking about Hobbies

(1) With "jouer (to play)"

The verb "jouer (to play)" has many usages, some of which require prepositions between the verb and the object. Here are two fixed usage for playing sports or games and musical instruments with "jouer".

Usage
Example

jouer + à + sport/game

Jouer au football

to play soccer

Je joue aux cartes.

I play cards.

Nous n’aimons pas jouer aux échecs.

We don’t like playing chess.

jouer + de + musical instrument

Jouer de la guitare

to play the guitar

Elle joue du piano.

She plays the piano.

Adrien aime le jazz, il joue de la guitare.

Adrien likes jazz, he plays the guitar.


(2) With "faire (do)"

Another way to say "play a sport" is by using "faire de", which is the same as "jouer à + sport".

Example

faire du basketball

to play basketball

Mon petit ami fait du tennis.

My boyfriend plays tennis.

Aimez-vous faire du sport ?

Do you like playing sports?


2. Plural Nouns for Hobbies

We have learned that certain nouns are customarily used in plural. This holds true for hobbies as well.

n. (sg.)
Hobby

Carte

card

jouer aux cartes

to play cards

échec

chess

jouer aux échecs

to play chess


3. Present Progressive

We can describe actions currently in progress in the present tense or use the phrase "en train de + infinitive".

(1) With the present tense: 

Example

Je joue à l’ordinateur.

I’m playing on the computer.

Mon petit ami fait du tennis.

My boyfriend is playing tennis.


(2) With "être en train de + infinitive" 

Example

Je suis en train de travailler.

I’m working.

Mon chien est en train de me regarder.

My dog is looking at me.



4. "Faire"-Questions: Present or Present Progressive?

When you ask "what does one do" in French, you might get two types of answers: what one does for a living and what one is doing at the moment. To differentiate, you can add adverbs like "maintenant" to emphasize "now".

Question
Reply

Que fait-il ?

What does he do?/What is he doing?

Il est musicien.

He’s a musician.

Il chante.

He’s singing.

Que fait-il maintenant ?

What is he doing now?

Il joue de la guitare.

He’s playing the guitar.

Learning Tips for Ability 1

1. Verb Conjugation in the Present Tense: "Savoir" (to know/can)

Savoir

je sais

I know/can

tu sais

you know/can

il/elle sait

he/she/it knows/can

nous savons

we know/can

you know/can

ils/elles savent

they know/can


2. Using "Savoir"

"Savoir" can mean "to know" and "can/to be able to".

The message it conveys is determined by what part of speech it goes with.

Usage
Example

savoir + n.

to know

Mon chat sait le chemin.

My cat knows the way.


savoir + inf.

can/to be able to




Je sais jouer du piano.

I can play the piano.

Ce poisson sait nager, ce coq aussi !

This fish can swim, so can this rooster!

Mon père sait bien skier.

My father can ski well.

Ma mère ne sait pas faire la cuisine.

My mother can’t cook.

used alone: 

savoir

to know

Je sais.

I know.

Qui sait…

Who knows…


3. Des + Plural Adjective = De

When the indefinite article "des" comes before an adjective in its plural form, "des" will become "de". 

However, this applies to only one circumstance where the adjective is BEFORE the noun.

Example

de jolies filles

some pretty girls

de jeunes étudiants 

some young students

In the following example, "des" does not become "de" because the adjective succeeds the modified noun.

Wrong: Ce sont de musiciens connus.

Correct: Ce sont des musiciens connus.

3000036.Value:
Learning Tips for Comparison

  1. Comparatives
  2. Superlatives
  3. Masculine to Feminine: Certain Adjectives Ending in "-s"


1. Comparatives

The basic structures of comparatives are fixed:

A is more...

plus/moins + adj.

Je suis plus grand.

I’m taller.

Tu es moins grand.

You’re shorter.

(lit. You are less tall.)

A is more…than B

plus/moins + adj. + que + B

Je suis plus grand que mon frère.

I’m taller than my brother.

Mon frère est moins grand que moi.

My brother is shorter (less tall) than me.

Attention:

The comparatives of "bon" (good) and "mauvais" (bad) are irregular, just like "better" and "worse".

"Better" is "meilleur" and "worse" is "pire".

Example

Ce restaurant est meilleur.

This restaurant is better.

Ce n’est pas une nouvelle mauvaise. C'est pire.

That's not a piece of bad news. This is worse.


2. Superlatives

1) Superlatives of one-syllable and qualitative adjectives:

definite article + plus/moins + adj. + n.

Example

Le plus petit singe

the smallest monkey

Ma femme est la plus belle femme.

My wife is the most beautiful woman.

La Russie est le plus grand pays du monde.

Russia is the largest country in the world.

2) Superlatives of multiple-syllable adjectives :

definite article + n. + definite article + plus/moins + adj.

Example

La fille la plus intelligente

the most intelligent girl

Tu es la personne la plus importante pour moi.

You are the most important person to me.

C’est l’histoire la plus intéressante.

This is the most interesting story.


3. Masculine to Feminine: Certain Adjectives Ending in "-s"

Some adjectives ending in "-s" change the ending to "-sse" when they modify feminine nouns.

m.
f.

bas

low

basse

low

gros

big/large/fat

grosse

big/large/fat

2. Transitive-Indirect Verbs

In French, there are transitive-direct and transitive-indirect verbs. The former take direct objects whereas the latter need prepositions to connect the verb and the object.

We will have a look at a few transitive-indirect ones:

Transitive-Indirect Verb
Example

répondre à

to answer

Je réponds aux questions du professeur.

I answer/am answering the teacher’s questions.

nuire à

to harm

Ton préjugé nuit à notre développement.

veiller sur

to care about/watch over

3000038.Value:
Learning Tips for Emotions

Part I (Lesson 1)

  1. Preposition "pour" (for)
  2. Verb "Penser" (to think)
  3. Positions of Stressed Personal Pronouns

Part II (Lesson 2~3)

  1. Être + adj. + de + inf...
  2. Pourquoi?
  3. Adverb "Si" (so)


Part I

1. Preposition "Pour" (for)

"Pour" tells us the purpose of an action. It can take nouns or infinitives.

Example

Je suis contente pour toi.

I am happy for you.

Elle vient ici pour me voir.

She comes here to see me.


2. Verb "Penser" (to think)

"penser + à" means "to think about, to remember". We can use either stressed pronouns or the verb infinitive after "penser + à".

Penser + à

Je pense à toi.

I'm thinking about you.

Pense à envoyer la lettre!

Remember to send the letter!



Part II

1. Être + adj. + de + inf. ...

This expression formula means "to be...to do/for something".

Let’s see some examples to get a better idea:

Example

Je suis content de te voir.

I am happy to see you.

Il est triste de ne pas passer l’examen.

He is upset about not passing the exam.

Nous ne sommes pas content d’aller à l’école.

We are not happy about going to school.

Attention: If we want to negate the part after "de", "ne pas" comes BEFORE the infinitive. (see the 2nd example)


2. Pourquoi?

We use "pourquoi" to ask "why", and "parce que" to give the response. 

Question
Answer

Pourquoi tu manges toujours du chocolat ?

Why do you always eat chocolate?

Parce que j’aime le chocolat.

Because I like chocolates.

Pourquoi tu n’es pas à l’école ?

Why aren’t you at school?

Parce que nous sommes dimanche.

Because it’s Sunday.

Tu es à Paris, pourquoi ?

You’re in Paris, why?

Parce que j’ai un travail là-bas.

Because I have a job there.

Il ne m’aime pas, pourquoi?!

He doesn’t love me, why?!

Parce que il l'aime.

Because he loves her.


3. Adverb "Si" (so)

Example

Si jolie!

So pretty!

Tu es si intelligent !

You are so smart!

3000042.Value:
Learning Tips for Future Tense 1

Near Future Tense

The near future tense in French is used to describe events that are going to take place very soon.

The verb form in the near future tense is constructed by "aller" (to go), which conjugates in the present tense, and verb infinitives.

"aller + faire"

Example

Je vais aller à Toulouse.

I'm going to Toulouse.

Nous allons voyager en Italie.

We're going to travel in Italy.

Les élèves vont finir ces examens.

The students are going to finish these exams.

1. Recent Past Tense

Verbs in the French recent past are formed this way:

venir + de + faire

The recent past is used to talk about something that just happened in the recent past.

Example

Je viens d’aller à Toulouse.

I just went to Toulouse.

Nous venons de voyager en Italie.

We just went to travel in Italy.

Les élèves viennent de finir ces examens.

The students just finished these exams.


2. Preposition "à"

The preposition "à" indicates the flavor of food:

Example

gâteau au chocolat

chocolate cake

glace à la pomme

2. Using indirect object pronouns

Indirect object pronouns are placed before the verb.

Correct

Tu veux me présenter ton professeur ?

Do you want to introduce your teacher to me?

Attention:

a) The indirect and direct object pronouns for "je/nous/tu/vous" look identical.

For example:

  • Je te regarde. ("te", direct object pronoun)
  • Je te réponds. ("te", indirect object pronoun, "répondre à" is a complete phrase, and "à" is no longer needed with indirect object pronouns)
