3000046.Value:
Learning Tips for Ability 2

  1. Verb Conjugation in the Present Tense: "Pouvoir" (can/to be able to)
  2. How To Use "Pouvoir"
  3. "Comme" (because/as/since)
  4. Distinguishing "Savoir" and "Pouvoir"
  5. Expressions of Ability
  6. Contractions: Direct Object Pronouns

1. Verb Conjugation in the Present Tense: "Pouvoir" (can/to be able to)

Pouvoir

je peux

I can

tu peux

you can

il/elle peut

he/she/it can

nous pouvons

we can

vous pouvez

you can

ils/elles peuvent

they can


2. How To Use "Pouvoir"

To express the ability or possibility to do something: pouvoir + inf.

Example

Je peux parler français.

I can speak French. 

Tu ne peux pas fumer.

You can't smoke here.


3. "Comme" (because/as/since)

"Comme" can lead a complete statement to give reasons for something. "Comme" is placed at the beginning of a sentence.

Example

Comme je suis Français, je sais parler français.

Since I'm French, I know how to speak French. 

Comme il fait beau, nous pouvons aller au parc.

Since the weather is nice, we can go to the park. 

Attention :

"Car", "comme" and "parce que" all can mean "because", but their positions in a sentence differ.

  • "Car" is put in the middle of a sentence;
  • "Comme" is at the beginning of a sentence;
  • "Parce que" is used only to respond to a pourquoi-question and is placed at the beginning of a statement.
Example

Je prends le train, car Paris est loin d’ici.

I'm taking the train, because Paris is far from here.

Comme Paris est loin d’ici, je prends le train.

Because Paris is far from here, I'm taking the train.

Pourquoi tu prends le train ?

Parce que Paris est loin d’ici.

Why do you take the train?

Because Paris is far from here.


4. "Savoir" vs. "Pouvoir"

"Savoir" leans a talent or skill, and "pouvoir" emphasizes the ability enabled by the environment.

For example, you know how to dance (savoir), but due to physical injury, you can’t dance (pouvoir) at the moment.

Example

Je sais parler français.

I know how to speak French.

(I have the capability to speak the French language.)

Je peux parler français.

I can speak French.

(I have the option to speak French.)


5. Expressions of Ability

Expression
Example

arriver à faire

can do something

J’arrive à finir ce travail.

I can finish this job.

être capable de faire

be capable of doing something

Je suis capable de passer cet examen.

I'm capable of passing the exam. 

avoir du mal à faire

have trouble doing something

J’ai du mal à manger des poissons.

I have trouble eating fish.


6. Contractions: Direct Object Pronouns

You may have noticed that the direct object pronouns "le/les" look the same as the definite articles "le/les".

We know that the definite articles contract with certain prepositions, "de" and "à", for instance. However, when direct object pronouns "le/les" are after "de/à", they do not contract with articles or prepositions.

le
definite article
Je veux boire du(de+le) vin.

I want to drink wine.

direct object pronoun

Je suis capable de le finir.

I am capable of finishing it.

les

definite article

Elle va aux(à+les) Etats-Unis.

She's going to the US.

direct object pronoun

Elle a du mal à les finir.

She has trouble finishing them.

Learning Tips for Infinitives

  1. Infinitives
  2. Avant de (before)
  3. Causative with "Faire"


1. Infinitives

An infinitive is the basic form of a verb without any inflection.

A French infinitive can function as either a verb or a noun. Even without a personal pronoun, an infinitive can be at work perfectly.

Example

Vouloir, c’est pouvoir.

Where there is a will, there is a way (will is power).

Avant de venir ici, tu dois finir ton travail.

Before coming here, you should finish your job.

Tu dois penser à boire du lait.

You should remember drinking milk.

Il est facile de passer cet examen.

It's easy to pass this exam.


2. Avant De (before) 

avant de + inf.

Example

Avant d’aller en France, tu veux apprendre le français ?

Before going to France, do you want to learn French?

Avant de venir ici, tu dois finir ton travail.

Before coming here, you should finish your job.


3. Causative with "faire"

There are two ways to say "have/make someone do something" in French. 

The first one: Subject + faire + inf. + Agent  (Subject makes the agent do something)

Learning Tips for Relative Clauses 1

Part I (Lesson 1)

  1. Relative Pronoun "Qui"
  2. Emphasizing Using Relative Pronouns

Part II (Lesson 2)

  1. Relative Pronoun "Que"
  2. Object Pronoun Order

Part III (Lesson 3)

  1. Relative Pronoun "Où" and "D’où"
  2. Beaucoup de的使用


Part I

1. Relative Pronoun "Qui"

"Qui" (who/that) can refer to either people or objects. 

In a relative clause where "qui" is the introductory word, "qui" acts as the subject. 

Its form will never change, but, same as in English, the verb in the relative clause must be in the appropriate form that agrees in both gender and number with the subject in the main clause.

Example

Adrien est un musicien qui parle français.

Adrien is a musician who speaks French.

Les filles qui regardent papa sont mes sœurs.

The girls who are looking at dad are my sisters.

C’est nous qui voulons acheter ces chaussures.

It's us who want to buy these shoes.


2. Emphasizing Using Relative Pronouns

This sentence structure, "c’est X + relative pronoun + clause", puts emphasis on the "X" part, resembling "it is…who/that…".

Example

C’est moi qui passe cet examen.

It's me who passes this exam.

C’est lui que j’aime.

It's him whom I love.

C’est cette ville où j’habite.

This is the city where I live.


Part II

1. Relative Pronoun "Que"

In a relative clause introduced by "que" (that), it functions as the direct object, which can be either people or objects.

Example

C’est le stylo que je veux acheter.

This is the pen that I want to buy.

La question que tu me poses est difficile.

The question that you raised to me is difficult.

Les cadeaux que je t’offre sont chers.

The gifts that I'm giving you are expensive.


2. Object Pronoun Order

When direct and indirect object pronouns appear at the same time, some pronouns take precedence over others. The rank is shown below:

1st
2nd
3rd

me

te

nous

vous

le

la

les

lui

leur



Example
Translation

Je le lui dis.

I'm telling him about it.

Il veut me l’offrir.

He wants to give it to me.


Part III

1. Relative Pronoun "Où" and "D’où"

"Où" (where) can refer to only places or time.

Example

C’est la ville où j’habite.

This is the city where I live.

C’est l’école où elle fait ses études.

This is the school where she studies.

C’est le bureau où je travaille.

This is the office where I work.


When the noun "où" points to is preceded by the preposition "de", we must keep "de". Then the relative pronoun would become "d’où".

Example

C’est la ville d’où je viens.

This is the city where I come from.

C’est l’école d’où elle vient.

This is the school where she comes from.


2. Beaucoup de的使用

"Beaucoup de" means "many/a lot". It precedes plural nouns without any articles.

Example

beaucoup d’étudiants

a lot of students

J’ai beaucoup de lunettes.

I have a lot of glasses.

Learning Tips for Future Tense 2


1. The Simple Future

We have learned one tense that indicates the future, namely the near future, which can be applied only to actions that are about to occur very soon.

In this unit, we are having our focus on the simple future, which covers a larger scope in terms of future events.

The simple future verb form

When the infinitive ends in "-e", we take out "-e" and add the endings above.

Some verbs are irregular. These verbs get new verb stems in the simple future form and then take the endings listed above.

Using the simple future

As said before, the scope of the simple future is much broader than the near future. 

The simple future can be used to talk about any events that have not yet taken place, whether it is a party to be held tomorrow or a trip planned for next year.

Example

J’arriverai dans deux jours.

I will arrive in two days.

Je serai en France.

I will be in France.

Elle m’enverra un mail.

She will send me a mail.


2. Preposition "Dans" (in)

"Dans" paired with time indicates "in how much time", referring to a future point of time.

Example

dans une heure

in an hour

J’arriverai dans trois jours.

I will arrive in 3 days.

Il sera là-bas dans une semaine.

He will arrive in one week.

3000050.Value:
Learning Tips for House Renting

  1. Distance
  2. Relative Pronoun "dont"
  3. Area


1. Distance

être loin/proche de... = to be far from/near...

Example

L’arrêt est proche du supermarché.

The station is close to the supermarket.

Le parc est loin du supermarché.

The park is far from the supermarket.


2. Relative Pronoun "dont"

This one refers to possession, resembling the English "whose/of which". It is used to replace "de + person/object".

Without Relative Clause
"Dont" Relative Clause

Je veux louer un studio, le prix du studio est bon marché.

I want to rent a studio. Its price is cheap.

Je veux louer un studio dont le prix est bon marché.

I want to rent a studio whose price is cheap.

J’aime le salon, les fenêtres du salon sont grandes.

I like the living room. Its windows are large.

J’aime le salon dont les fenêtres sont grandes.

I like the living room whose windows are large.


3. Area

il fait + ...(number) = an area of...

Here, "il" is impersonal.

Example

Il fait trois mètres carrés.

It is 3 square meters large.

La chambre fait douze mètres carrés.

The room is 12 square meters large.


4. Preposition "de" for Larger Numbers

Example

Le loyer de cette villa est de deux mille euros.

The rent for this house is at 2000 euros.

Le prix de cette bague est de six cents euros.

The price of this ring is at 600 euros.

3000051.Value:
Learning Tips for Negation

  1. Expressions of Negation
  2. Verb "Voir" (to see)
  3. "Si" in Negation


1. Expressions of Negation

Expression
Example

ne...ni...ni

neither...nor...

Je ne suis ni pauvre ni riche.

I'm neither poor nor rich.

sans

without

un café sans sucre

 a coffee without sugar

ne...pas du tout

not...at all

Je ne sais pas du tout.

I don't know at all.

ne...guère

not much

Il ne vient guère chez moi.

He doesn't come to my pace much.


2. Verb "Voir" (to see)

One way of using "voir" is no different from the "see" in "I see", which is to show comprehension.

Example

Je vois.

I see.

Tu vois, il n’arrive jamais à l’heure.

You see, he never comes on time.


3. "Si" in Negation

"Si" and "non" both express negation.

Their difference is, "non" negates an affirmative statement whereas "si" confirms a negative statement.

Question
Reply

Es-tu Français ?

Are you French?

Non, je ne suis pas Français.

No, I'm not French.

Tu n’es pas Français ?

Are you not French?

Non, je ne suis pas Français.

No, I'm not French

Si, je suis Français.

Yes, I'm French.

Est-elle policière ?

Is she a police?

Non, elle n’est pas policière.

No, she's not a police.

Elle n’est pas policière ?

Is she not a police?

Non, elle n’est pas policière.

No, she's not a police.

Si, elle est policière.

Yes, she's a police.

3000052.Value:
Learning Tips for Past Tense 2

  1. The Passé Composé 1
  2. Past Participle Formation
  3. Affirmation and negation in the passé composé
  4. Il y a + Time = How Long Ago
  5. Pronoun/Adjective "Tout"


1. The Passé Composé 1

The passé composé can be translated into the simple past tense or the present perfect tense in English.

The passé composé requires auxiliary verbs, "avoir/être",  and past participles:

avoir/être (conjugated in the present tense) + past participle

The majority of French verbs take "avoir" in the passé composé and only a certain group of verbs take "être". 

Here, we will concentrate on the conjugation of past participles first and verbs that take "avoir". In the unit "Past Tense 3", we will go into detail about when to use "avoir" and when "être".


2. Past Participle Formation

Verb Type
Formation Rule

Regular "-er"ending verbs

Substitute "-er" with "é".

e.g. "aimer" becomes "aimé"

Regular "-ir"ending verbs

Substitute "-ir" with "i".

e.g. "finir" becomes "fini"

Irregular verbs (the third verb group)

It depends, no predictable pattern, usually the ending would be one of "i/is/it/u", but it’s not hard-and-fast either.


We are listing the past participles of a few irregular verbs here:

Infinitive
Past Participle
Ending

avoir

to have

eu

Irregular

être

to be

été

devoir

should

dû

to see

vu

lire

to read

lu

to want

voulu

can

pu

to wait

attendu

boire

to drink

bu

prendre

to do/take/eat

pris

-is

apprendre

to learn

appris

dire

to say

dit

-it

écrire

to write

écrit

faire

to do/make

fait


3. Affirmation and negation in the passé composé

Affirmative statements are constructed by applying the "avoir + past participle" formula.

Affirmative Statement

J’ai écrit une lettre.

I wrote a letter.

Il a bu du lait.

He drank the milk.

Nous avons fait du sport hier.

We played sports here yesterday.

In negative statements, "ne…pas" surrounds the auxiliary verb, not the past participle.

Wrong: J’ai ne vu pas Léo.

Correct : Je n’ai pas vu Léo.

(I didn’t see Léo.)

Gender and Number Agreement in the passé composé

When there are direct object pronouns in the passé composé, the past participle needs to agree with the pronoun in both gender and number.

Example

Je l’ai vu hier.

I saw her yesterday.

Nous les avons fini.

We finished them.

Ces fleurs sont jolies, mon chat les a volées.

These flowers are pretty. My cat stole them. 


4. Il y a + Time = How Long Ago

"Il y a + time" appears very frequently in passé composé.

Example

J’ai passé cet examen il y a une semaine.

I passed this exam a week ago.

Elles ont visité les États-Unis il y a un an.

They visited the US a year ago.

Je t’ai écrit ce mail il y a trois jours.

I wrote you this letter 3 days ago.


5. Pronoun/Adjective "Tout"

As a pronoun, "tout" means "all/everything".

As an adjective, "tout" measn "all/whole", and this is how it is used: tout + definite article + n.

Exampletout (adj.)

toute la soirée

the whole night

tout le monde

the whole world

J’ai vu toute sa famille.

I saw his whole family.

Learning Tips for Passive

1. Present Passive

Only transitive-direct verbs (transitive verbs that take direct objects) can be made passive.

A + être + past participle + par/de + B

(A is done by B)

Attention: 

The past participle must agree in both gender and number with the subject "A".

Let’s take a look at a comparison between the active and the passive:

ActivePassive

Julie invite Adrien.

(present)

Julie invites Adrien.

Adrien est invité par Julie.

Adrien is invited by Julie.

Adrien invite Julie.

(present)

Adrien invites Julie.

Julie est invitée par Adrien.

Julie is invited by Adrien.

Adrien a invité Julie.

(passé composé)

Adrien invited Julie.

Julie a été invitée par Adrien.

Julie was invited by Adrien.


Je suis aussi contente d’être invitée.

(infinitive)

I'm glad to be invited.（the speaker is female）

Who/what performs the action can be introduced by "par/de".

For action verbs, we use "par";

For abstract verbs such as "to like/feel/hate", we use "de" to introduce the action doer.

PrepositionExample

par

Mon frère est battu par ma sœur. 

My brother is beat by my siter.

Je suis vu par ce policier.

I am seen by the police.

de

M. Dupont est respecté des étudiants.

Mr. Dupont is respected by the students.

Je suis aimée de ce garçon.

I am loved by the boy.

Attention :

  1. "De" contracts with the definite articles "le/les".
  2. When the action doer led by "par/de" is a personal pronoun, we use a stressed one.
Example

Je suis vu par vous.

I am seen by you(pl.)

Elle est aimée de lui.

She's loved by him.

Learning Tips for Reflexive Verbs

  1. Reflexive Pronouns
  2. Reflexive Verbs


1. Reflexive Pronouns

A verb is reflexive when the action is performed on the doer.  Reflexive verbs always take the reflexive pronoun that agrees with the subject: reflexive pronoun + verb infinitive

Reflexive pronoun table

Reflexive pronouns do not differ in gender.

Subject Pronoun

Grammar function of reflexive pronouns

Grammar-wise, a reflexive pronoun can function as a direct object or an indirect object depending on whether the reflexive verb it is paired with is a transitive-direct one or a transitive-indirect one.


2. Reflexive Verbs

Besides indicating that the subject and the object are the same, reflexive verbs can also be used in a mutual sense to mean "each other".

Indicating that the subject and the object are the same:

Example

Je me promène.

I go for a walk. 

("promener" means "to walk", "to walk yourself" is to take a walk)

Il se présente.

He introduces himself.

"Each other"

Example

Nous nous aimons.

We love each other.

Ils se connaissent.

They know each other.

3000055.Value:
Learning Tips for My Day

  1. Reflexive Verbs in the Passé Composé
  2. Gerunds
  3. Present participle
  4. Using gerunds


1. Reflexive Verbs in the Passé Composé

Reflexive verbs use "être" to build the passé composé:

subject + reflexive pronoun + être + past participle

When the reflexive pronoun functions as the direct object of the reflexive verb, the verb’s past participle must agree in gender and number with the pronoun.

Example

Je me suis levée à six heures.

I got up at 6.

(I’m a girl)

Nous nous sommes couchés à dix heures du soir.

We went to bed at 10 at night.

When the reflexive pronoun functions as the indirect object of the reflexive verb, the verb’s past participle does not need to agree in the gender and number of the pronoun.

Example

Je me suis lavé les mains.

I washed my hands.

(The reflexive verb acts upon the hands, not me)

Il s’est brossé les dents.

He brushed the teeth.

(The reflexive verb acts upon the teeth, not him)


2. Gerunds

A gerund is the noun form of a verb. It can be used to express two simultaneous actions.

In French, a gerund = en + present participle


3. Present participle

We will explain how to form a present participle:

Replace the ons-endings of verbs in the "nous"-conjugation with "-ant":

For example,

Nous parlons (we speak) => parlant

Nous finissons (we finish) => finissant

Nous faisons (we do) => faisant

Now we have our present participles: "parlant", "finissant" and "faisant".


4. Using gerunds

As said in the beginning, the gerund is used on two concurrent actions.

Example

Ce chanteur chante en dansant.

The male singer sings while dancing.

Je fais des devoirs en écoutant de la musique.

I do homework while listening to music.

Nous travaillons en attendant les vacances.

We work while waiting for the holiday.

3000056.Value:
Learning Tips for Past Tense 3

  1. The Passé Composé 2
  2. Grâce à
  3. H Aspiré


1. The Passé Composé 2

As mentioned before, the majority of French verbs take the auxiliary verb "avoir" to build the passé composé.

Now, we are tackling the construction with "être".

Many intransitive verbs have "être" as their auxiliary verb in the passé composé. A lot of those verbs indicate motion or changes from one condition to another. Also, reflexive verbs take "être" to build their passé composé.

The past participle following "être" agrees in both gender and number with the sentence subject.

The following are verbs that take "être" to build the passé composé we learned in this unit:

Infinitive
Past Participle
Example

aller

to go

allé

Je suis allé à Paris.

I've gone to Paris.

venir

to come

venu

Tu es déjà venue à Nantes ?

Have you come to Nantes?

partir

to leave

parti

Nous sommes partis hier.

We left yesterday.

rentrer

to return

rentré

Il est rentré chez lui.

He returned to his home.

tomber

to fall

tombé

Je suis tombé amoureux de lui.

I fell in love with him.

naître

to be born

né

Ce héros est né en deux mille.

This hero was born in 2000.

mourir

to die

mort

Mes poissons sont morts.

My fish died.

décéder

to pass away

décédé

Cette personne âgée est décédée.

The old person passed away.

devenir

to become

devenu

Oh mon dieu, mon petit ami est devenu une fille.

OMG, my boyfriend became a girl. 


2. Grâce à

Grâce à + n./subject pronoun = thanks to...

Example

Grâce à lui, j’ai passé cet examen.

Thanks to him, I passed the exam.

Grâce à ton aide, je peux voyager en France.

Thanks to your help, I can travel in France.

Grâce à l’aide de mon professeur, je suis devenue une bonne élève.

Thanks to my teacher's help, I became a good student.


3. H Aspiré

Unlike H muet that requires the elision, H Aspiré does not require it.

For example, the h in "héros" is H Aspiré. So, this is how we should write it: Le héros. NOT L’héros.

Learning Tips for Adverbial Pronouns

Part I (Lesson 1)

  1. Adverbial Pronoun "En"
  2. Inversion of "Il y a"

Part II (Lesson 2)

  1. Adverbial Pronoun "y"

Part III (Lesson 3)

  1. The Word Order of Pronouns
  2. The Position of Pronouns


Part I

1. Adverbial Pronoun "En"

There are two adverbial pronouns in French, "en" is one of them.

The purpose of using adverbial pronouns is to tidy up the sentence, avoiding unnecessary repetition.

Generally, "en" is placed before the verb.

The following is "en" in use:

1) Number + n. : "en" replaces the noun

Question

Combien de bonbons as-tu ?
How many candies do you have?

Answer

J’en ai trois.
 I have three.
 (en=trois bonbons)

Il en a beaucoup.
He has a lot.
 (en=beaucoup de bonbons)

Attention: The number must remain.

2) "En" replaces indefinite articles/partitive articles

Question

As-tu du vin ?
Do you have wine? 

Answer

J’en ai.
I have.
(en=du vin)


3) "En" replaces indirect objects introduced by "de"

Question

Parlez-vous de la cuisine chinoise ?
Are you talking about the Chinese cuisine? 

Answer

Oui, nous en parlons. 
Yes, we are talking about it.
(en=parlons de la cuisine chinoise)


4) "En" replaces the "de + X" part in "adj. + de + X"

Example
La cuisine de ce restaurant est très bonne, nous en sommes satisfaits.
The food of this restaurant is very good. We are very satisfied with it.
(en=satisfaits de la cuisine)

 

2. Inversion of "Il y a"

When the sentence structure "il y a" is reversed in questions, it's: y a-t-il.

Question
Answer

Y a-t-il un lit chez toi ?

Do you have a bed at your place?

Oui, il y en a un chez moi.

Yes, there is one at my place.


Part II

1. Adverbial Pronoun "y"

The other adverbial pronoun is "y". Generally, "y" is placed before the verb.

1) To replace any adverbial phrase with "à/chez/dans/devant/sur + place/location":

Example

Tu es au bar, j’y suis aussi. 
 You're at the bar. I'm in it, too.
(y = au bar)

J’aime ce roman, j’y ai trouvé des choses intéressantes.
I like this novel. I found something interesting in it.
(y = dans ce roman)

2) To replace any indirect object led by "à" except humans:

Example

Cette question est difficile, j’y réfléchis. （y=réfléchis à cette quesiton）

This question is hard. I'm thinking about it.



Cette lettre, il faut y répondre. （y=répondre à cette lettre）

This letter, it should be answered. 

Attention : "Y" can’t be used to refer to humans. 

Wrong : Je parle à cet homme, tu y parle aussi ?

Correct : Je parle à cet homme, tu lui parle aussi ? (I'm talking to this man. You're talking to him, too?)

2. The Position of Pronouns

Direct object pronouns, indirect object pronouns, reflexive pronouns, and adverbial pronouns all precede the main verb.

However, they are placed AFTER the modal verb.

Example

Je peux l’aider.

I can help him.

Tu veux y aller ?

Do you want to go there?

Je ne dois pas te l’expliquer.

I shouldn't explain it to you.

Learning Tips for Imperatives


1. Constructing the Imperatives

The imperative mood conveys a strong proposal or command. Only pronouns "nous", "tu" and "vous" have imperatives.

How do we construct imperatives?

  1. Leave out the subject of the verb in a statement.
  2. For reflexive verbs, leave out the subject but keep the reflexive pronoun.
  3. In a positive statement, the reflexive pronoun will be put after the verb with a hyphen linking the pronoun to the verb. The reflexive pronoun "te" will become "toi".

Imperatives can be used only as a main clause or as an individual clause, never as a subordinating clause.

3000060.Value:
Learning Tips for Past Tense 4

  1. The Imperfect
  2. The Imperfect in Use
  3. The Pluperfect


1. The Imperfect

The imperfect tense is used to talk about ongoing, habitual, regular or uncompleted actions in the past. It is different from the passé coposé, which emphasizes the completed actions.

To conjugate verbs under the imperfect: take the verb in its present tense nous-conjugation and replace the suffix with the followings:

1) For "-er" ending regular verbs/the first verb group:

habiter （to live)

j’habitais

nous habitions

tu habitais

vous habitiez

il/elle habitait

ils/elles habitaient

2) For "-ir" ending regular verbs/the second verb group:

finir（to finish）

je finissais

nous finissions

tu finissais

vous finissiez

il/elle finissait

ils/elles finissaient

3) For irregular verbs/the third verb group:

avoir（to have）

j’avais

nous avions

tu avais

vous aviez 

il/elle avait

ils/elles avaient


être（to be）

je étais

nous étions

tu étais

vous étiez

il/elle était

ils/elles étaient


2. The Imperfect in Use

There are several ways of applying the imperfect. In this unit, we are looking at two.

a)  Describing an ongoing action that lasted for a period of time in the past:

Example

Je fumais beaucoup.

I used to smoke a lot.

Nous habitions en France.

We used to live in France.

b)  Describing a recurring, habitual action in the past:

Example

Je me levais à six heures.

I used to get up at six.

Il allait à l’école à huit heures.

He used to go to school at eight.

c)  The imperfect vs. The passé composé

Example

Quand je jouais à l’ordinateur, mon père est entré dans ma chambre.

When I was playing on the computer, my dad came into my room.

(ongoing action: me playing on the computer; momentary action: my dad coming in)

Quand je suis arrivé chez moi, il pleuvait encore.

When I arrived home, it was still raining.

(ongoing: raining; momentary: me arriving home)


3. The Pluperfect

The French pluperfect corresponds to the English past perfect, referring to actions that had happened prior to a past event.

How is it formed?

On the basis of the passé composé, the past participle stays the way it is, the auxiliary verb changes from the present to the imperfect.

Example

Quand je suis rentré chez moi, papa avait fait la cuisine.

When I returned home, my dad had made the meal.

Il m’a dit qu’il avait fini son travail.

He told me he had finished his work.

3000061.Value:
Learning Tips for Health

1.  Expressing Pain

To say where you have pain: "avoir mal à + body part"

Example

J’ai mal au ventre.

I have a stomache.

Nous avons mal à l’estomac.

We have stomachaches.

Il a mal aux dents.

He has a toothache.

3000062.Value:
Learning Tips for Conditional


1. The Present Conditional

We use the conditional to talk about something that may or may not occur. The conditional can also express a possibility, a wish or a suggestion.

How is it formed?

The present conditional verb form = the simple future verb stem + the imperfect verb ending

Infinitive
Simple Future
Present Conditional

aimer

J’aimerai

J’aimerais

sortir

Tu sortiras

Tu sortirais

être

Il sera

Il serait

aller

Nous irons

Nous irions

finir

Vous finirez

Vous finiriez

pouvoir

Ils pourront

Ils pourraient


2. "Si" clause with Present Conditional

"Si (if)" often introduces a conditional clause. 

The "si" clause uses the imperfect tense, and the main clause uses the present conditional.

Example

 Si je te demandais plus de questions, serais-tu mécontente ?

If I would ask you more questions, would you be unhappy?

S’il pleuvait, ils ne pourraient pas jouer au basketball.

If it would rain, they wouldn't be able to play basketball.


3. "Si" clause with Past Conditional

The past conditional can be used to talk about hypotheses about the past.

The "si" clause uses the pluperfect, and the main clause in past conditional. 

The past conditional = auxiliary verb conjugated in present conditional + past participle

Example

Si tu avais été moi, tu m’aurais compris.

Had you been me, you would have understood me.

(It’s impossible for you to be me)

Si j’avais gagné beaucoup d’argent, je l’aurais partagé avec toi.

Had I made a lot of money, I would have shared it with you.

(In reality, I didn't make a lot of money)

Learning Tips for Exclamation

  1. Exclamations
  2. Tellement (so...that.../too...to...)
  3. En tant que (as)


1.  Exclamations

There are certain words that are necessary to form exclamations.

Structure 1: Exclamation word + adj.

Exclamation Word
Example

Quel

Quel beau paysage !

What a beautiful view!

Quelle bonne employée !

What a great empoyee(f.) !

Si

Tu es si intelligent !

You are so intelligent!

Mon patron est si gentil !

My boss is so nice!

Structure 2: Exclamation word + sentence

Exclamation Word
Example

Comme

Comme LingoDeer est utile !

How useful LingoDeer is !

Comment

Comment la chambre est horrible !

How horrible the bedroom is!

Que

Qu’il est adorable !

How adorable he is!

Si

Si j’avais su !

If only I had known!

(the past conditional here expresses pity)


2. Tellement (so...that.../too...to...)

"Tellement" is an adverb. Here is how we use it in sentences:

Subject + verb + tellement + infinitive/adj. + que + statement

Example

Ta chambre est tellement sale que je ne veux pas y entrer.

Your bedroom is so dirty that I don't want to enter.

J’ai tellement soif que je pourrais boire la mer.

I am so thirsty that I can drink the ocean.


3. En tant que (as)

The noun following this expression phrase does not take any articles.

For example:

En tant que professeur (as a teacher)

En tant que patron (as a boss)

1. Using the Present Conditional

We can use the present conditional to make polite requests or give suggestions:

Example

Aurais-tu du temps ?

Would you have time?

Pourriez- vous me l’expliquer ?

Could YOU explain it to me?

Pourrais-je vous déranger ?

Could I bother YOU?


2. Asking for Directions

We can use the question word "comment" to ask for directions.

Example

Comment aller au supermarché ?

How can I get to the supermarket?

Comment je peux aller à la Tour Eiffel ?

How can I get to the Eiffel Tower?

  2. Present Subjunctive in Use

1. Present Subjunctive

Subjunctives express the speaker’s subjective judgment/opinions.

The subjunctive always appears after "que (that)". 

A present subjunctive verb form = the stem of the present ils/elles-verb conjugation + the following subjunctive endings:

Infinitive
ils/elles
conjugation
Present Subjunctive

to pass

2. Present Subjunctive in Use

1) Exclamation:

Vive la France. (Long live France.)

2)  Hope:

J’espère que tu passes cet examen.  (I hope that you pass this exam.)

3) Polite requests:

Je vous prie que vous m’aidiez. (I beg that you would help me.)

4) Imperative

Il demande que nous entrions. (He demands that we come in.)

5) Emotions

Je suis contente que tu viennes. (I’m glad that you are coming.)


3.  Expletive "Ne"

The expletive "ne" does not have any actual meaning. 

It needs to be added to a subjunctive clause when we want to express negative emotions such as doubt, disappointment or fear.

Example

J’ai peur que tu ne reviennes pas.

I'm afriad that you wouldn't come back.

J’ai peur que tu ne me reviennes.

I'm afraid that you would come back to me.

Elle est déçue que vous ne mentissiez.

She's disappointed that you(pl.) would lie.

2. The Subjunctive in Use

The subjunctive is necessary when making an impersonal statement starting with "il faut.../it vaut mieux". 

For example:

Il faut que tu travailles. (You should work. / lit. trans: It's necessary that you work.)

Il vaut mieux que vous passiez cet examen. (You’d better pass this exam./ lit. trans: it's better that you pass this exam.)

3000070.Value:
Learning Tips for Expressions

1. The Subjunctive 3

In this unit, we will learn more ways to use the subjunctive.

The subjunctive can express the followings:

1) Purpose:

Pour que tu sois heureux, j’ai décidé de te quitter. (For you to be happy, I have decided to leave you.)

2) Condition:

Pourvu que tu viennes me voir, je t’offrirais un cadeau. (As long as you would come see me, I would give you a present.)

À moins que tu ne sois malade, tu dois aller à l’école. (Unless you are sick, you should go to the school.)

3) Concession:

Qui que tu sois, je t’aimes. (No matter who you are, I love you.)

Où que vous êtes, nous vous suivons. (No matter where you are, we would be following you.)

4) Time:

Avant que vous n’ailliez en France, vous devez obtenir votre visa. (Before you arrive in France, you should obtain your visas.)

Attention :

"Avant que" and "à moins que" both require the expletive "ne".

The expletive "ne" doesn’t have any actual meaning; it exists only to make the sentence grammatically correct.

3000077.Value:
Learning Tips for Simple Present

  1. Simple Present
  2. Verb Conjugation: "Manger" (to eat)

1. Simple Present

The French simple present tense does what the English simple present tense and present progressive tense do. It can express a current state-of-being, an ongoing action or an action that is about to occur.

For example, "Je mange une pomme" can be interpreted as either "I eat an apple" (eating an apple is my habitual routine), or "I am eating an apple (now)".

There is not a grammatical construction in French that corresponds to the "-ing" in English. But we will learn other ways to emphasize a current action in the future.

2. Verb Conjugation: "Manger" (to eat)

In this unit, we will focus on verb conjugation in present tense: "manger" (to eat)
